articles.title: Rules Deep Dive: Core Concepts
articles.html:
This article is our deep dive on the Core Concepts section of the 11th Edition rules, focusing on what’s changed in the new edition.

This piece is aimed primarily at existing players, and we’re going to focus on what’s changed rather than digging into the full mechanics of the phase. If you’re a brand new player looking to dive into 11th Edition, we recommend you go and take a look at our main review, as that provides a full outline of how Warhammer 40,000 works on the tabletop!

As a note - for our review, we have been provided with access to the extended digital rules that will be made generally available in the Warhammer 40,000 app. This means that in a few places, we may talk about a rule or specific nuance that hasn’t been covered in previews or things you’ve seen elsewhere. We’ll try to flag the important ones!

Huge thanks to Games Workshop for providing us with review copies of all the material covered today. Please note that any digital material we refer to is subject to change until it is made available on Warhammer Community or the Warhammer 40k App.

Other Articles§

If this isn’t the part of the rules you were looking for, or you’re wondering what to look at next, check out the links to all our deep dive articles below:

  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Concepts
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Command Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Moving and the Movement Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Attacks and the Shooting Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: The Charge and Fight Phases
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Terrain and Objectives
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Special Unit Types
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Stratagems and Abilities

What’s the Same§

We’ll start with what hasn’t changed since 10th Ed, and the answer is plenty. All of the key building blocks of 10th Edition Warhammer 40,000 are still here. Even if they’ve changed a bit, concepts like Battleshock, Invulnerable Saves, Coherency and Engagement remain, and the set of phases that make up a turn is identical.

The Core Concepts section of the rulebook also takes you through definitions of key concepts like Armies, units, and models, none of which have functionally changed, plus how you measure distances and roll dice.

You’ll find a similar pattern as we go through all the phases and elements of the game - almost everything you’re used to is here in some shape or form, but plenty of it has changed, and 11th introduces some whole new mechanics like Hidden that will add new dynamics to the game.

What’s Changed in Core Concepts?§

There’s quite a few meaningful changes here that will have some major impacts on how you play the game. To start, we can talk about how the rules themselves are laid out. Rules are now broken out into blocks, each with a number designation which makes referencing said rules much easier. This was implemented in the new edition of AoS with a lot of success so it's no surprise to see it here.

The downside is there are quite a few rules that are only in the app. While I’m someone whose core book lives on their shelf the nanosecond after it gets home, having rules just not be complete feels a bit off and it's somewhat odd not to just have a digital ruleset all together if you’re gonna go this route. There’re these little tabs on the page that’ll tell you to go download the 40k app for more info on various topics, which sure does feel like they could have just put that stuff on the page.

Wings: I think the kinder reading on this is that quite a few of the rules that are app only are things where they want more freedom to tune or iterate the exact wording, or make functional changes over time. I’d agree there are a few strange absences however, perhaps the most glaring being that the rules for Heal, a rules term used without in-line explanation in some of the previewed stratagems, don’t appear in the rulebook.

From here, we have a bit of a scattershot of topics, so we’ll just address them one at a time.

Rules Presentation§

Various different groups of rules (e.g. types of move, Actions, types of shooting, Stratagems) have each been given a standard template that they consistently follow. These make the process and conditions for the rules very clear, and help ensure that identical verbiage is used each time. Some of these rules are also “modal” - you will be provided with multiple options to choose between at the start, and then have different instructions to follow as you proceed through them. An example of this is Disembarking - this is a type of move, and uses the template for this, but within it there are three different modes, covering the two types of Disembark you could do in 10th Edition (before or after moving), and a new one that’s been added in 11th.

Active and Opposing Player and Sequencing§

This is a concept that's existed for a bit that’s a bit more codified now. If it’s your turn, you’re considered the “Active Player” which generally means you get to choose how certain interactions stack up if things trigger at the same time. Now though, if you’re in the middle of a move on your opponent’s turn or selected to shoot or fight you become the active player until those actions have resolved.

The digital rules also outline how the active/opposing players sequence rules that have identical timing (e.g. "at the end of the movement phase").

First, the active player does all their mandatory abilities, then selects and activates all their optional abilities. After that, the Opposing Player does the same. If any new rules are triggered by things either player does, those do not happen until all pre-existing rules have resolved.

This cleans up some of the more nebulous parts of Warhammer 40K, and also takes away an issue 10th had, which was that the Active Player always chose the sequencing of rules, and thus could sometimes neutralise potentially powerful combos simply by declaring the order they wanted. Now, the opposing player usually has the last say on any given trigger, letting them respond effectively.

Invulnerable Saves§

Invulnerable saves are largely unchanged. They’re still an unmodifiable save that you use in place of your normal save. The big change though is that they are no longer optional. There were times last edition where it was advantageous to use your invulnerable or normal save in order to better force a unit of yours to die. Now you check the value of the roll first against your invulnerable save and then your normal save to see if damage is inflicted.

Turn Structure§

Turns and phases now have a defined Start and End step along with a defined scoring sub-step. Functionally, this won’t change anything for your games, but things can now get much more specific with timing to fix race conditions. This may get more involved once new books start coming out and rules reference these specific sub-steps.

Hazard Rolls§

Hazard rolls are a new core concept that will be familiar to those who used Hazardous weapons last edition. Hazard rolls are forced by certain rules like weapons with the HAZARDOUS tag, desperate escapes and emergency disembarkation from transports. The effect is simple, you roll a D6 and on a 1-2 you take a mortal wound or 3 if you’re a Monster or Vehicle. This means rules that interact with Hazard rolls now have a lot more breadth of impact than just your plasma getting hot, as well as making your desperate escapes slightly less desperate (though those now have other drawbacks).

Actions Are Back in the Core Rules§

Thank god. There was some weirdness in the last edition with actions never being properly codified in the core rules which meant that it had to be reprinted with every new Chapter Approved release. Actions get a new structure too. Each Action gets a defined Start, Unit field (who can do it), Use Limit, Completes (when it's done) and Effect field, making them slightly easier to read to find the relevant information you’re looking for.

To perform an Action, a unit must:

  • Have at least OC1
  • Be on the Battlefield
  • Not be an Aircraft/Fortification
  • Not have Advanced/Fallen Back this turn.
  • Not have started another Action this turn.

In addition, all types of shooting prevent a unit being eligible to start an Action for the rest of the turn, and the reverse is also true; If you start an action, then for the rest of the turn, you are not eligible to Shoot, and are also not eligible to Charge.

Titanic models can all do actions when engaged and can shoot and do actions but otherwise they’re the same as 10th’s last iteration of them.

Battle-shock§

Battle-shock is also ever so slightly different! You still roll 2d6 and check to see if it's below your leadership, but now on a failure your OC becomes “-” instead of 0. The null value means it can’t be modified after the fact, which was the case before. You also check to see if you need to make such a check at Half-Strength, meaning 2 model units are no longer immune. Lastly, and this is likely the biggest change, you must take another Battle-shock test in your next command phase to see if you become un-Battle-shocked. This means abilities that force a test in your turn gain a ton of value, since it means there's another chance for the unit to stay Battle-shocked before scoring.

Army Construction§

Army construction features in the extended app rules only, and has some changes from 10th Edition. Much of it will be familiar - you still select an Army Faction, points limits are the same, you still have a limit of three of each Datasheet (except Dedicated Transports and Battleline, which are doubled). However, there's some new things as well:

  • Rather than picking a single Detachment, you now have a pool of Detachment Points to spend, allowing you to take more than one. You get three of these at 2000pts.
  • Joining Characters to units happens during army construction, not before the battle, so you cannot flex your Leaders between different units.
  • Some Leaders have now become Support Characters instead. These work the same way as Leaders, but must be joined to a unit, so cannot be taken independently. You can attack one Leader and one Support Character to a single unit, so this centralises "double attachment" rules.
  • Picking Enhancements for your units happens after attaching Leaders/Support, and each unit can only take one, which means you cannot combine two Enhancements in the same squad.
  • Some Enhancements are now Upgrades. These can be taken on non-Character units, and each Upgrade can be taken up to three times while only using a single Enhancement pick, though you do still have to pay for each. As far as we can tell, they do still count towards the one Enhancement per unit limit, so you cannot combine them with something on a Leader.
  • Your Warlord must now have the same Faction keyword as your Army Faction. This mostly affects some occasional Imperium armies that would use an allied Inquisitor Draxus as their Warlord.

Modifiers§

The digital rules includes details of how modifiers apply, and what limit you can modify different Characteristics to, much of which is going to be familiar to existing players. However, everyone's favourite rule, the one for applying multiple modifiers (most commonly to damage), now gets rewritten, and will hopefully make things play more naturally.

When applying multiple modifiers to a characteristic, you do so in this order:

  • Apply anything that sets a specific value.
    • If you set a value to 0 or - at this step, stop and do not process any further modifiers.
  • Apply multiplication
  • Apply addition
  • Apply division
  • Apply subtraction
  • Round any fractions up

For those not au-fait with the nuances of intricate rules interactions from previous editions, this has three common impacts:

  1. Effects that set damage to 0 no longer get partially bypassed by +D effects. Here they now do nothing.
  2. Effects that half damage no longer get bypassed by Melta. You now add the damage, then halve it. This also removes the weird edge case that against a weapon doing 1+1 damage, you were better off with a -1D effect than a half damage effect.
  3. Buffs that provide +1OC no longer bypass Battle-shock, both because it now sets that value to "-", and even if it did still set the value to 0, you'd ignore further modifiers.

There are also modification limits for the new Detection Distance mechanic of Hidden, and also on Lone Operative range (Lone Operative can be be Lone Operative X"). Effects cannot result these being to be better than 9" or worse than 30". This is a nerf for the small amount of what are functionally Lone Operative 6" effects out there.

Wrap Up§

That’s it for the Core Concepts. If you want to read more about the rules, go check out our other deep dives from the links up top, or if you’re interested in models, missions or our overall opinions on 11th Edition, head back to the site’s front page, and you’ll find it all there.

articles.title: Rules Deep Dive: Command Phase
articles.html:
This article is our deep dive on the Command Phase in 11th Edition, focusing on what’s changed in the new Edition.

This piece is aimed primarily at existing players, and we’re going to focus on what’s changed rather than digging into the full mechanics of the phase. If you’re a brand new player looking to dive into 11th Edition, we recommend you go and take a look at our main review, as that provides a full outline of how Warhammer 40,000 works on the tabletop!

As a note - for our review, we have been provided with access to the extended digital rules that will be made generally available in the Warhammer 40,000 app. This means that in a few places, we may talk about a rule or specific nuance that hasn’t been covered in previews or things you’ve seen elsewhere. We’ll try to flag the important ones!

Huge thanks to Games Workshop for providing us with review copies of all the material covered today. Please note that any digital material we refer to is subject to change until it is made available on Warhammer Community or the Warhammer 40k App.

Other Articles§

If this isn’t the part of the rules you were looking for, or you’re wondering what to look at next, check out the links to all our deep dive articles below:

  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Concepts
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Command Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Moving and the Movement Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Attacks and the Shooting Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: The Charge and Fight Phases
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Terrain and Objectives
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Special Unit Types
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Stratagems and Abilities

The Command Phase§

Each player’s turn begins with a Command Phase, where players stock up on resources, activate special abilities, and check whether the morale of their injured units holds up to the punishment they’ve taken. A few things have changed here, most notably that morale is now a bit more fragile, and more likely to have a major impact on your games.

Start and End of Phase§

Since this is the first phase we’re covering, it’s the first places you’ll notice that all phases now have an explicit “Start of Phase” and “End of Phase” step, where abilities that trigger or are activated at this point occur. Both of these are governed by the new sequencing rules, so in each of these sub-steps, the active player (whose turn it is) resolves all mandatory rules (in the order they choose), then all their optional abilities, after which the opposing player does the same. This means that the opposing player always gets the “final say” on activating optional effects in any phase. That’s more relevant for phases like Movement (where there’s now no window for the active player to do anything after a Rapid Ingress), but worth bringing up here.

Mission Rules at the End of the Phase§

The End of the Command Phase step has a sub-step for scoring missions, which always happens after all other End of the Phase effects are processed. This means that scoring is always evaluated based on the final state after all the dust of special abilities has settled.

Command Abilities Step§

Everything in the Command Phase that doesn’t happen either at the start or end of the phase, or in the Battle-shock step (which we’ll talk about in a moment) now happens in a dedicated step. Importantly, this is after Battle-shock, so any effects that happen in the Command Phase that return models to units will now happen after evaluating whether the unit needs to test its morale. This weakens the resistance of units with reliable access to re-spawn effects (and indeed if they’re Stratagem-based and you fail Battle-shock, then you’re totally out of luck).

Battle-shock§

Right, Battle-shock. This is the biggest change in the Command Phase, because getting Battle-shocked in 11th Edition is both easier and worse than in 10th Edition. In the Battle-shock step of the Command Phase, any unit that is at or below Half-Strength has to take a test against its Leadership, and if it fails it is Battle-shocked. Battle-shocked units have their Objective Control characteristic set to a value of “-” (an unmodifiable zero), can’t be targeted with Stratagems, and cannot perform any Actions (and fail any that were ongoing).

The first two changes are there in that paragraph - you now take Battle-shock if you are at Half-Strength, not just below it, which removes the oddity in 10th Edition that units which started with two models in them would never have to test (and makes such tests much more common for 4W solo models, the other type of unit that almost never cared). The other is that rather than setting your OC to 0 (which could then be modified back up with something like an Ancient’s banner), the stat goes to “-”, so there’s nothing you can do to salvage things.

The other new threat here is that, as well as units that are at or below Half-Strength, you also have to test for any units that started the Phase Battle-shocked. No more automatic recovery - you now have to get it together with a passed Leadership test, and with almost all Ld values being between 6 and 8, this is never a sure thing. In addition - the Insane Bravery Stratagem is still available to automatically pass a Battle-shock test once per game, but you can’t use that on a unit that’s already Battle-shocked, so anything trying to recover is rolling the dice.

This immediately ups the value of every single effect in the game that can inflict Battle-shock on enemy units, and makes a few models like Commander Dante who do so in a radius complete nightmares; they can potentially shatter an army’s cohesion for a prolonged period all by themselves. The converse, units that provide the ability to remove Battle-shock like a Royal Warden, also shoot up in priority when building lists. In addition, this is a major nerf to Transports, because being forced to make an Emergency Disembark still automatically Battle-shocks a unit, and they might not recover to hold whatever objective they landed on now!

Expect Battle-shock to have far more impact on your games, and stock up on tokens for tracking it.

Wrap Up§

That’s it for the Command Phase. If you want to read more about the rules, go check out our other deep dives from the links up top, or if you’re interested in models, missions or our overall opinions on 11th Edition, head back to the site’s front page, and you’ll find it all there.

articles.title: Rules Deep Dive: Moving and the Movement Phase
articles.html:
This article is our deep dive on the Movement Phase in 11th Edition, focusing on what’s changed in the new Edition.

This piece is aimed primarily at existing players, and we’re going to focus on what’s changed rather than digging into the full mechanics of the phase. If you’re a brand new player looking to dive into 11th Edition, we recommend you go and take a look at our main review, as that provides a full outline of how Warhammer 40,000 works on the tabletop!

As a note - for our review, we have been provided with access to the extended digital rules that will be made generally available in the Warhammer 40,000 app. This means that in a few places, we may talk about a rule or specific nuance that hasn’t been covered in previews or things you’ve seen elsewhere. We’ll try to flag the important ones!

Huge thanks to Games Workshop for providing us with review copies of all the material covered today. Please note that any digital material we refer to is subject to change until it is made available on Warhammer Community or the Warhammer 40k App.

Other Articles§

If this isn’t the part of the rules you were looking for, or you’re wondering what to look at next, check out the links to all our deep dive articles below:

  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Concepts
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Command Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Moving and the Movement Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Attacks and the Shooting Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: The Charge and Fight Phases
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Terrain and Objectives
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Special Unit Types
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Stratagems and Abilities

Unit Positioning§

The fundamentals of how units are positioned on the battlefield get two big changes in 11th Edition, which are going to be some of the biggest adjustments for returning players.

Coherency§

Any time a unit ends a move or is set up, it must be in Coherency, which features one of the biggest changes in 11th. Units are now in Coherency if every model is within 2” of at least one other model in the unit, and every model is within 9” of every other model in the unit. Essentially, draw a 9” diameter circle on the table, and every model’s base must be touching some part of it.

This means that stringing out a large unit in a giant conga line is fully dead, and even 5-model units can’t occupy quite as much space as they used to. This is a fairly heavy blow to horde lists, as it reduces the amount of trouble they can cause with one large squad, both in terms of move blocking and starting huge fights. It also makes fully screening the table much more difficult, especially in combination with a lower general Ingress range of 8” instead of 9”. For some mid-sized melee units, there can be some upside to this - it’s a bit easier to deploy with models pushed out in multiple directions to keep charge options open, and the new Charge and Engagement rules are generally pretty good for letting 10-ish model units maximise their Fighting.

Engagement§

Speaking of Engagement Range, this is now globally changed to 2” horizontal distance (still 5” vertically) but there’s no longer any restriction on moving through enemy Engagement Range with “standard” move types, as long as you end the move outside them. This takes some getting used to, as players have gotten very good at eyeballing 1” distances, and tactically it has the impact that it’s easier for a relatively small enemy unit to completely prevent you from moving onto an objective from a given direction, even though some of the objective footprints are bigger now. That can make melee threats a real tough balancing act for shooting armies to handle - if you want to flip their objective, you’ve got to leave enough alive that you can Charge, but not enough that they’ll then murder you. Alternatively, you can use an ability that lets you move after shooting, and those probably go up in value for just this reason!

Larger engagement can also have an impact on setting up out of Transports, but the rules here have been changed quite a bit to make it harder (though probably not impossible, read on later) to trap foes inside. On that note, all Set Up rules require models to be placed outside Engagement Range of enemies unless otherwise specified. Finally, Engaged is now a rules term - a model is Engaged if it is within range of one or more enemy models, and any unit that has any models within it Engaged is also said to be Engaged. Conversely, any unit to which this does not apply is Unengaged.

Moving Models§

11th updates the general rules for how models move, bringing some updates and changes that were added gradually to 10th into the Core Rules, and also streamlining a few other elements.

Types of Move§

A lot of rules in 11th Edition are presented as types of move, in a fancy new rules template. This includes the expected Movement Phase options like Normal Moves or Advances, but also covers Charges, Fight Phase Movement, and things that used to have their own category of rules like Disembarking from Transports or arriving from Reserves as an Ingress Move.

Each type of move will either include a Maximum Distance, for types of move that where models travel around the Battlefield, or a Set-Up Distance, which applies to types that Set Up a unit. Maximum Distance governs how far a unit can travel, while Set-Up Distance will be measured relative to some position, and determine where models must be placed.

Types of Move will also often have the following sets of conditions and/or triggers on them:

  • Before Moving: Decisions you need to make before starting the move.
  • While Moving: Restrictions you must follow while moving/placing individual models.
  • After Moving: Conditions the whole unit must fulfil after all models have been moved, plus anything that triggers at this point.

In addition to any After Moving conditions specific to the move, at that point the unit must also be in Coherency.

For all types of move that use a Maximum Distance, you can always choose to move one or more of the models in your units. This can be relevant for moves like Charges that apply quite strict While and After moving conditions - if you don’t want a model to be pulled into an awkward position, you can leave it in place, as long as you can meet While Moving limitations for the models you do choose to move, and After Moving conditions for the unit.

Importantly, however, you sometimes won’t be able to do this - While Moving conditions are applied first while models are moving, and Coherency is only checked at the After Moving step, and if not met, the entire unit’s move is undone.

This means that if a move, for example, requires a model that moves to end in engaged with an an enemy unit, and doing so would pull them out of coherency with a model you don’t want to move, then your choice is to either move both models so that you meet the While Moving conditions for the first one, or leave the whole unit stationary - you can’t use the “threat” of coherency to sandbag on your obligations during the While Moving step.

Straight Lines and Pivots§

All movement is now executed as a series of straight lines and pivots, up to the total movement that the type of move you’re making allows. If, for example, a large model needs to move around a corner, you need to measure a straight line from its current position long enough to clear the back of its base/hull past the corner, then a second straight line to rocket it off in a new direction. This largely formalises how people already measured moves like this, but makes it very clear and explicit.

The bigger change is in pivots. When you pivot a model, you rotate it around its centre axis, and this does not cost you any movement. This can potentially add a good 1-2” of reach on especially large models if they start with their wider axis facing the foe, though that can make them trickier to hide. We assume that the reason that pivot costs are gone is that it no longer causes issues with changing Charge distances due to the new ways those are resolved, but you do need to be aware of potential extra reach from normal movement.

There are also no more diagonal FLYing moves, because…

FLY§

FLY gets a rework in 11th Edition, bringing it much closer to how it worked in older Editions. The FLY keyword now does nothing at baseline, not even letting you move through enemy models, but when making a Normal/Advance/Fall Back/Charge Move they can Take to the Skies. When they do, they subtract 2” from their maximum move distance, but can then freely move through all enemy models and all terrain for the duration of that move. In addition, they can ignore vertical distance when moving, so if they want to hop up to a higher floor in a ruin, there’s no additional cost beyond the 2”.

This is generally a massive boost for Monsters/Vehicles/Mounted units with FLY, and a minor downgrade (in most situations) for Infantry/Beast units. The latter could already move through walls, and now need to pay 2” to hop enemy models. The former can now push out from terrain far more quickly, putting some classic elf nonsense back on the table (and juicing up many big centrepiece monsters). The reduction in big, solid ruins in the official terrain set makes it slightly less of a step change than it could have been, but it’s still very good, and if any event packs stick to larger walls like current UKTC or WTC ones it’s going to be monstrously strong.

Moving Through Gaps§

Models that have a base can move through any space their base fits through, so you can ignore overhanging tentacles/reaper chainswords or whatever. This was added as rules errata in 10th, and is now a locked-in part of the core rules.

Monsters and Vehicles§

Monsters and Vehicles can now move through non-Monster/Vehicle enemy models when making Normal or Advance moves, as long as they end the move legally (so usually outside Engagement Range). Clearing Engagement Range is not trivial now that it’s 2” on either side, so this is mostly for faster models in the Movement Phase, but with a good Advance you can sometimes hop to a surprising position.

Monsters and Vehicles are also now affected by the FRAME keyword, which is the rules signifier for “measure to hull”. If such a model has no base, when pivoting it you rotate it around its central axis. There are a couple of weird edge cases here with some units that have bases and also FRAME which we expect to get cleared up in FAQ and Commentary.

Moving Through Terrain§

We’ll cover this in the Terrain Deep Dive, so look for it there!

Movement Phase§

11th Edition makes a few key changes to the flow of how the Movement Phase is executed.

Start and End of Phase Steps§

Like every Phase, the Movement Phase now has explicit Start and End of Phase steps, where abilities which trigger at these points are resolved. These are also governed by the new sequencing rules, which mean that all Active Player abilities are resolved before all Opposing Player ones.

No Reinforcements Step§

Structurally, the biggest change to the Movement Phase is that there’s no separate Reinforcements step. At the start of the Movement Phase, all units in your army, including those in Reserves or Transports, are eligible to make a Move, and where they are determines the types that they are eligible to make. Models in Reserves are only eligible to either Remain Stationary or make an Ingress Move, the latter of which is how you set up from reserves. You can resolve these at any point in the Movement Phase, which can have a small number of fringe applications if you have some sort of unit with a Movement buff aura waiting in the wings.

More on Ingress Moves and Transports further down.

Note: Some rules in 10th Edition Codexes specify that they take place in the Reinforcements step of the Movement Phase, so you may be wondering where these now happen. The extended version of the rules that’s going into the App includes a catch-all for these that they now happen in the Move Units step of this phase.

Overwatch Is at the End of the Phase§

Necron Hexmark Destroyer

We’ll cover this in more detail in the Core Stratagems and Abilities article, but Overwatch is no longer a reaction to movement - it’s now triggered in the End of the Movement Phase step. In terms of Movement Phase flow, this speeds things up considerably, because you no longer have to ask whether a foe is Overwatching each time you move, nor try to brain genius the sequencing to stop them doing so.

Types of Move in the Movement Phase§

11th Edition now has six types of move that units can make in your Movement Phase. These are:

  • Remain Stationary
  • Normal Move
  • Advance Move
  • Fall Back Move
  • Ingress Move
  • Disembark move

Four of these return from 10th Edition, and will be covered here, while Ingress Moves and Disembark Moves get their own sections below.

Each move type is laid out with the new 11th Edition templating, making it very clear what the eligibility, process and limitation for each is. One thing we’ll shout out here - perhaps the only thing we don’t like about these templates is that the distance limit is in a grey bar at the top, which the eye can skim over. Check that if you’re baffled where (for example) the bonus for Advancing lives!

Remain Stationary§

You do nothing. Pretty simple. The rules do make it clear that “nothing” includes no pivoting (that’s free during a move, but it’s still moving), and Remaining Stationary does not trigger any “ends a move” effects. In addition, any unit in Reserves or Transports that does not want to Ingress/Disembark is now Remaining Stationary.

Normal Move§

An unengaged unit can move up to their M” Characteristic, and can act normally for the rest of the turn. Simple, classic, unchanged from 10th.

Advance Move§

An unengaged unit can move up to their M” Characteristic +d6”, and afterwards are not eligible to perform an Action or Charge for the rest of the turn (and when Shooting, will only be able to select Assault Shooting by default). Once again, functionally unchanged from 11th, just clearer wording.

Fall-Back Move§

Fall-Back moves have the biggest change in 11th Edition, as selecting between a “normal” Fall Back (now called an Ordered Retreat) and a Desperate Escape. Fall Back is the only move type other than Remain Stationary you can select while Engaged, and is how you leave combat.

An Ordered Retreat can be selected if a unit is not currently Battle-shocked, and allows them to move up their M” Characteristic, following all normal rules for movement. After completing this move, the unit is not eligible to Shoot, perform an Action, or Charge for the rest of the turn.

If you either cannot select Ordered Retreat because your unit is Battle-shocked, or need to move through an enemy model in order to escape combat, you can select a Desperate Escape. This allows a unit to move up to their M” Characteristic, and move through enemy models while doing so, but every model in the unit must make a Hazard roll, and if the unit is not already Battle-shocked, they have to take a Battle-shock test afterwards.

This changes the calculus on Desperate Escape quite a bit from 10th. On the one hand, it’s now less directly lethal to Monsters and Vehicles, who will never just die unless they were already on the brink, and multi-wound Infantry also suffer less casualties per dice rolled. On the flip side, the whole unit now has to take Hazard rolls even if only one model needs to Escape, and the Battle-shock test has the potential to throw plans into disarray. In the aggregate, wrapping enemy models is scarier again now, so dust-off that tri-pointing technique!

Ingress Moves and Reserves§

11th Edition introduces the term Ingress Move to cover the process of a unit arriving on the Battlefield from Reserves, or being repositioned by teleportation or similar.

What Is an Ingress Move?§

An Ingress Move formalises the process of arriving on the battlefield during it. Models in Strategic Reserves can make a default Ingress Move, which allows them to be set up on the Battlefield:

  • In Coherency.
  • >8” Horizontally from all enemy units.
  • Wholly within 6” of a battlefield edge.
  • Not within your opponent’s Deployment Zone if it is before the third Battle Round.

The Deep Strike ability now works by modifying the restrictions on an Ingress Move, allowing you to be set up anywhere on the Battlefield that is >8” from enemy units, without the Battlefield Edge or Deployment Zone limitations.

Newly authored abilities that reposition a unit also use Ingress Moves. An example of this is the new Weirdboy Datasheet in the Armageddon box, where Da Jump works by removing a unit from the Battlefield, placing them in Reserves, then immediately allowing them to make an Ingress move.

No Shooting Phase Movement After Ingress§

A big change in 11th Edition is a return to the status quo of pre-10th Editions: if you make an Ingress Move, then until the next Charge Phase you are not eligible to make any type of Move. This means that you cannot arrive, blast something, then use an ability allowing movement after shooting to scoot back behind a wall (or sweep forward to claim an objective). We were, if we’re honest, never super clear why anything thought it was a good idea for this limitation to go away in 10th, but this will be a somewhat painful (re)adjustment for some mobile factions.

Strategic Reserves Limits§

Just like in the previous two Editions, you can choose for part of your army to begin in Reserves even if they don’t have Deep Strike or any other ability. What’s new in 11th is that there’s now just a single 1000pts limit on this (or half your army, for other game sizes), no separation between Deep Strike and Strategic Reserves.

Limitations on when Strategic Reserves can arrive are now part of the Core Rules as well - no Ingress Moves before the second Battle Round unless otherwise stated, and any unit that starts the game in Strategic Reserves that has not been set up by the end of Battle Round 3 is Destroyed (excluding units that are embarked in a Transport that has arrived from Reserves, they just haven’t Disembarked yet).

Repositioned Units§

Units placed into Reserves during the battle now have a blanket name - Repositioned Units. Somewhere my Editor is hoping, praying that this means he will never have to read “uppy-downy” used as a term of art ever again. Repositioned units are exempt from the requirement that units in Reserves must arrive by Battle Round 3, and have some sensible safety net clarifications about how ongoing effects work with them.

Transports§

Many armies have cool trucks (or cultural equivalents) that their forces can ride around in. 11th Edition makes some important changes to how these work. Transports still have a Transport Capacity that determines how many models and of what kind can ride in them at any given time.

Embarking§

By default, a unit that finishes a Normal, Advance or Fall Back Move with every model within 3” of a Transport can Embark within it as long as there is sufficient spare Transport Capacity to accommodate all the unit’s models.

However, there is a very important new limitation on this - you cannot do this (by default) if you were Set Up on the Battlefield this turn. Since Disembarking from a Transport involves being set up, this means that Fire and Fade type abilities now globally do not work to let you get out, shoot, then jump back in. Grey Knight Interceptors in absolute shambles. No more gradually adding this limitation to more and more Stratagems/Abilities via FAQ - it’s just a Core Rule.

Disembarking§

Disembark is now a type of move, and uses the modal ability template to provide three different options. Two of these systematise what you could do in 10th Edition with some tweaks, one is entirely new.

Because these are now types of Move, they “use up” your unit’s Move for the turn, so unless otherwise specified, you cannot make another move after using them.

Your first option is a Rapid Disembark, which you are eligible to do on a unit Embarked in a Transport that has either made a Normal Move or Ingress Move this phase. You set up the unit wholly within 3” of the Transport, and they are not eligible to Charge this turn. If you did this after an Ingress, you must follow any set up restrictions that the Transport had to follow while making that move. This mostly just systematises 10th Edition Rules for disembarking after Moving/arriving from Reserves, but on the latter it tightens up the conditions by applying all the same limitations to the Disembarking unit. Previously, the distance from the enemy applied, but not other conditions, so you could (e.g.) disembark a unit onto an objective that was slightly more than 6” from the Battlefield Edge when arriving from Strategic Reserves.

Second is the Tactical Disembark, which you perform either before the Transport has been selected to move, or on a Transport that has Remained Stationary. Here, you set your unit up wholly within 3”, as above, then that unit can immediately also make a Normal Move or Advance Move. This is 11th’s version of Disembarking before moving, and is mostly the same, but requiring the unit to move immediately after the Disembark changes things a little, because they have to do their move with the vehicle still in its starting position. This means you can’t ever end up with a unit that Disembarked in a position that the vehicle was originally occupying, so you sometimes need to plan ahead with how you’re staging behind terrain to account for this.

In both of the above cases, if a model’s base is >3”, then when disembarking the condition for them is changed to just require that they be within 1” of the Transport. This can give a smidge of extra reach to models like Ghazghkull.

Our final option here is new - the Combat Disembark. You can only select this if neither of the above apply - you haven’t moved and it’s not possible to set up your unit within 3” of your Transport, usually because of enemy Engagement Range. In this case, you can instead set up your unit wholly within 6” of your Transport, and can be in Engagement Range of one or more enemy units that are currently Engaged with the Transport. This comes at a cost, of course - your whole unit has to make Hazard Rolls and the unit takes a Battle-shock test, so you probably won’t be giving your foe your best shot!

Emergency Disembark§

Emergency Disembarks happen when a Transport is destroyed, and allow the models within it to be Set Up. When doing so, they must be wholly within 6” of the Transport, Unengaged, and also as close to it as possible. This is a change from 10th, clearly designed to give units a bit of breathing room to avoid larger Engagement Ranges without giving them free reign to rocket boost into the distance. It does, notably, make it much harder to avoid being tagged in the situation where two enemies charge a Transport, and the first wrecks it. Although the spacing is quite generous, 2” engagement range on either side of enemies is a huge amount of zoning, and something we’ll be getting our diagram wizards to look at is whether there are some configurations where a couple of units (particularly on oval bike bases) operating in concert can sufficiently surround a normal-sized transport to make this disembark impossible, and obliterate the models within.

Even if that doesn’t happen, going through this process is traumatic, and the passengers must make Hazard Rolls, and are Battle-shocked (which is more of a downside now that it can persist through the next Command Phase).

Wrap Up§

Lots of changes here, and plenty for players to absorb, but a lot of what’s here is ultimately designed to simplify and streamline things, and we think once players adjust to 11th, the Movement Phase will play out quite a bit faster. The functional changes to Coherency and Engagement will also have big impacts on the game as a whole, as will some of the new limitations that prevent units from acting with such impunity.

That’s it for the Movement Phase. If you want to read more about the rules, go check out our other deep dives from the links up top, or if you’re interested in models, missions or our overall opinions on 11th Edition, head back to the site’s front page, and you’ll find it all there.

articles.title: Rules Deep Dive: Attacks and the Shooting Phase
articles.html:
This article is our deep dive on the Shooting Phase in 11th Edition, focusing on what’s changed in the new edition, and also covering changes to the process of making attacks that are relevant to the Fight Phase as well.

This piece is aimed primarily at existing players, and we’re going to focus on what’s changed rather than digging into the full mechanics of the phase. If you’re a brand new player looking to dive into 11th Edition, we recommend you go and take a look at our main review, as that provides a full outline of how Warhammer 40,000 works on the tabletop!

As a note - for our review, we have been provided with access to the extended digital rules that will be made generally available in the Warhammer 40,000 app. This means that in a few places, we may talk about a rule or specific nuance that hasn’t been covered in previews or things you’ve seen elsewhere. We’ll try to flag the important ones!

Huge thanks to Games Workshop for providing us with review copies of all the material covered today. Please note that any digital material we refer to is subject to change until it is made available on Warhammer Community or the Warhammer 40k App.

Other Articles§

If this isn’t the part of the rules you were looking for, or you’re wondering what to look at next, check out the links to all our deep dive articles below:

  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Concepts
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Command Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Moving and the Movement Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Attacks and the Shooting Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: The Charge and Fight Phases
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Terrain and Objectives
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Special Unit Types
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Stratagems and Abilities

Making Attacks§

One of the biggest enduring issues for the last three editions has been that the sequence for processing attacks didn’t fully work in batches. “Fast Dice” was provided as a recommended option in all three cases, and in 10th Edition some extra changes mostly made it always work, but you still sometimes had to slow down and roll attacks one at a time.

11th Edition fully overhauls this with a comprehensive new set of rules for allocating attacks that makes batch execution the default, and it’s one of the biggest improvements in the rules for our money.

Attack Sequence in 11th Edition§

Making attacks in 11th Edition works as follows:

  1. You select your unit’s targets. For ranged attacks, this needs to be a visible target in range of your unit, and you can split your unit’s weapons (for ranged attacks) or attacks (for melee) between multiple target units as long as they’re all eligible.
  2. You select one of the units that was targeted, and then group all attacks allocated to that unit which are identical (with the same BS/WS, S, AP and D, plus the same special rules) to execute as a group.
  3. Make hit rolls for that group of attacks. These work the same as in 10th - you have to beat your BS/WS score on a d6 to succeed.
  4. Make wound rolls for that group of attacks. Again, same as in 10th - compare the S to the T of the enemy unit, and roll against a target number determined by that.

So far, all the same, and at the end of this you (hopefully) have a pool of successful Wounds. Now the new allocation rules kick in.

  1. The enemy player separates their units into groups of models with identical defensive profiles, i.e. the same Save, Invulnerable Save and Wounds. Each Character goes in a separate group.
  2. The enemy puts those groups in an allocation order. Character groups have to go after non-Character groups, and any group containing a Wounded model has to go ahead of groups without.
  3. The enemy now rolls a saving throw for each successful Wound Roll, and then puts those dice in order from lowest roll to highest.
  4. These saving throws are now resolved against the groups in the allocation order, starting with the lowest dice rolls (i.e. the first dice that are executed are any 1s rolled against the first allocation group). If a saving throw is below both the model’s Save (modified by AP) and Invulnerable Save characteristic, the attack inflicts damage on that model. If a model runs out of Wounds, they are destroyed.
  5. You work through dice in order till you either run out of successful wounds, or run out of models to kill.

If there are still models alive in the unit, and you allocated other weapons to them, you then resolve those. After that, if other units were targeted, you move onto those, and repeat the above process.

There’s plenty to unpack here, but these are the big impacts:

  • If the target unit has uniform defensive profiles, this doesn’t really change anything - it just makes everything faster.
  • Units with mixed defensive profiles now have to fully plan their defensive ordering up front. It’s much harder to judiciously drop attacks on the perfect model one at a time.
  • You now often want to put models with worse defensive profiles first, so that they absorb any 1s and 2s, and then hopefully by the time you get to the better defences, the enemy has run out of attacks.
  • Leaders with 4+ Invulnerable saves are now meaningfully less likely to get killed by attacks spilling over from their unit, because if they have a bodyguard of any size, then lower rolls will hopefully be used up by the time attacks reach them.
  • A small number of special Invulnerable Saves are extremely swingy with this rule. Models like Makari, Archons and users of some Enhancements that provide conditional 2+ Invulnerable Saves are almost unkillable via spillover damage, but will also die instantly if swung at when not in a unit.

There are a few technical points worth highlighting too:

  • You do still need to slow down and process damage from attacks one at a time, either where the attack does random damage, or when hitting a model with a Feel No Pain.
  • Invulnerable Saves are no longer optional - you can’t choose not to take them, they’re just part of your statline, and used to check if an attack succeeds.
  • A targeted unit can switch up allocation order between different groups of attacks as long as they meet the criteria above, which can be possible as long as they’ve only lost whole models, and not left a multi-wound model lingering on the edge of death.

This generally ends up making mixed defensive profiles a bit less intrinsically valuable, particularly for units that had the option to tag in a single model with different stats, but can make some options that let you split defences down the middle more interesting. Bullgryn, for example, might find more value in splitting Slab and Brute shields 50:50, and then putting the higher Wound count and worse saves first in the allocation order to soak the bad dice against a particularly heavy onslaught.

Mortal Wounds§

Some attacks do Mortal Wounds as well as regular damage (mostly attacks with Devastating Wounds). These are now processed at the end of each group of identical attacks, rather than at the end of the whole unit’s attack sequence.

Some other effects can also cause Mortal Wounds. When allocating these, the rules for the order in which you do so largely match those for selecting allocation order, but outside of an attack sequence Mortal Wounds are applied one at a time, so each time a model is destroyed you do have the option to switch to a different type of model to take the next one.

Cover§

Shooting Attacks can be affected by Cover. We’ll get into how you gain cover in the Terrain Rules article, but for this one you need to know what it does, which is inflict a -1BS penalty to the firing unit. Because this is a BS modifier, it can stack with a -1 to Hit modifier as well, so any unit with access to a -1 to Hit effect that isn’t Stealth (which now just provides Cover) gets a lot better!

The Shooting Phase§

In the Shooting Phase, all your eligible units can shoot. By default, all your units that are on the battlefield and have not either shot or started an Action this turn are eligible, but some kinds of move (e.g. Fall-back) prevent them from doing so.

Each eligible unit can make attacks using one of four shooting modes, which are:

  • Normal Shooting
  • Assault Shooting
  • Close-quarters Shooting
  • Indirect Shooting

All types of shooting prevent you from being eligible to start an Action afterwards.

Normal Shooting§

This does exactly what it says on the tin - your unit makes attacks with their ranged weapons, following the sequence outlined above. They can only do this if they did not Advance this turn, and are not Engaged.

Assault Shooting§

You can select Assault Shooting if your unit Advanced and is not Engaged, and has one or more Assault Weapons. Your unit can shoot, but when doing so can only use Assault Weapons. This is pretty much identical to how Assault worked in the last few editions, but made into a Shooting mode to avoid weird rules lacunae around when shooting eligibility is checked.

Close-Quarters Shooting§

Close-Quarters Shooting combines the rules for Vehicles/Monsters shooting while in combat, and other units firing Pistols or Close-Quarters weapons. Your must be Engaged to select this mode, and if they’re not a Monster/Vehicle, must have at least one Close-Quarters/Pistol weapon.

When a Monster/Vehicle shoots in this mode, they can pick targets as normal, and can also pick any unit they’re engaged with as a target (unless firing a Blast weapon). However, when doing so they suffer -1 to Hit unless the weapon they are firing is Close-Quarters. Notably, because this is applying -1 to Hit, if they’re firing at an enemy unit in Cover they’ll be at -2 to Hit - and on top of this, the bonus for Heavy weapons no longer applies to units that are Engaged. This means that shooting out of combat can be a lot less effective than you’re used to in 10th Edition.

Other types of unit can also use this shooting mode, but when doing so they can only fire Close-Quarters weapons, and only at foes they are Engaged with. They don’t suffer any penalties for doing so.

Indirect Shooting§

Units that have one or more weapons with the Indirect Fire special rule can select this mode as long as they are unengaged and did not Advance.

When they do so, they can shoot their non-Indirect Fire weapons as normal, and their Indirect ones gain the ability to target foes that are not visible to them. However, this comes with a number of drawbacks, which apply to all Indirect Fire attacks made:

  • The foe gains Cover against the attack.
  • You cannot re-roll the hit roll.
  • An unmodified hit roll of anything other than a 6 always fails unless your unit Remained Stationary, and at least one friendly unit can see the target (essentially acting as a spotter). In that case, an unmodified hit roll of a 1-3 always fails instead.

These are extremely punishing, and make Indirect Fire less good than it has been in the last few editions. You have to do more work to get to hit on a 4+ than previously, and even if you do that, not being able to re-roll hits strips away the main option that’s previously been used to get around the penalties. The effect is especially notable on units that bring a mix of Indirect and regular shooting like Fortis Kill Teams - now to get the full benefit of the Indirect, you’d have to sacrifice the whole unit’s movement. Also of note is that if you choose this mode, the penalties apply to your Indirect Fire weapons even if they target a visible foe, so for the vanishingly small number of units that have multiple different ones, you generally don’t want to do this if one of them is going into a juicy visible target.

The only small upside for Indirect Fire weapons in this edition is that a lot of them sit in the AP-1/AP-2 space which really benefits from the change to Cover, so each hit you do land has more impact (and essentially anything with BS3+ sees that as pure upside as long as they have a spotter). That doesn’t stop this being a pretty drastic reduction in the overall power of this ability, however.

Wrap Up§

That’s it for the Shooting Phase. If you want to read more about the rules, go check out our other deep dives from the links up top, or if you’re interested in models, missions or our overall opinions on 11th Edition, head back to the site’s front page, and you’ll find it all there.

articles.title: Rules Deep Dive: The Charge and Fight Phases
articles.html:
This article is our deep dive on the Charge and Fight Phases in 11th Edition, focusing on what’s changed in the new Edition.

This piece is aimed primarily at existing players, and we’re going to focus on what’s changed rather than digging into the full mechanics of the phase. If you’re a brand new player looking to dive into 11th Edition, we recommend you go and take a look at our main review, as that provides a full outline of how Warhammer 40,000 works on the tabletop!

As a note - for our review, we have been provided with access to the extended digital rules that will be made generally available in the Warhammer 40,000 app. This means that in a few places, we may talk about a rule or specific nuance that hasn’t been covered in previews or things you’ve seen elsewhere. We’ll try to flag the important ones!

Huge thanks to Games Workshop for providing us with review copies of all the material covered today. Please note that any digital material we refer to is subject to change until it is made available on Warhammer Community or the Warhammer 40k App.

Other Articles§

If this isn’t the part of the rules you were looking for, or you’re wondering what to look at next, check out the links to all our deep dive articles below:

  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Concepts
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Command Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Moving and the Movement Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Attacks and the Shooting Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: The Charge and Fight Phases
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Terrain and Objectives
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Special Unit Types
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Stratagems and Abilities

The Charge Phase§

How a unit Charges has changed significantly in 11th Edition, now being much simpler and a bit more abstract in how it’s resolved.

The Charge Phase has a start and end of phase step like all other phases in 11th Edition.

Charging in 11th Edition§

The big difference for Charging in 11th Edition is that the success or failure of a Charge Roll is now determined solely by the distance between the two units. In your Charge phase, any number of your eligible units (by default, all your units that are unengaged, within 12” of one or more enemies, and didn’t Advance or Fall-Back this turn) can attempt a charge.

For each that does, you roll 2d6, and that gives you a maximum distance in inches. You can then pick one or more enemy units that are within the maximum distance of your unit to be your charge targets and attempt to execute a Charge Move to engage them.

When making a Charge Move, you move every model in your unit up to the maximum distance that you rolled, and at the end of that you must be engaged with all the charge targets you selected. In addition, each model must end the move closer to one of your charge targets, and follow the following additional constraints (in descending order of priority):

  • They must end within 1” of one of the charge targets if possible.
  • They must end within engagement range of one of the charge targets if possible.

You also cannot end the move engaged with any enemy units you did not pick as a charge target.

If you cannot achieve that with the movement you have (e.g. because you need to go the long way round a corner), then you cannot move. Units that successfully complete a Charge Move can Fight First in the following Fight Phase.

Lots of changes there, so let’s highlight them one at a time:

  1. You no longer have to pick your targets before making a Charge Roll - you can toss the dice and see what you have to work with. This takes a bit of risk/reward calculations of the phase away, which is a shame, but speeds things up a bit.
  2. Your required distance to try and make a charge move is solely determined point-to-point between the two units, and with 2” Engagement Range, if you succeed on the roll you essentially have 2” of slack to work with, so will often succeed. It also means that without a bonus to your charge, a double 1 is an automatic failure, as you can never start a charge within 2” of the foe.
  3. Charges out of Deep Strike/Strategic Reserves still need a 9 on the roll. The set up distance for these is now >8”, so you can never be within 8” of the target when setting up. This also makes the rare effects that allow reduced setup distance that don’t prevent charges (e.g. Deathshroud Terminators) slightly worse (unless they also get changed) because the charges are essentially 1” harder.
  4. Staying an inch back from a wall no longer protects you from being charged by Monsters etc. With 2” Engagement range, they can swing a long way through obstacles.
  5. You don’t have to base your enemy, only get within 1”. This can be used to tee up some clever pile-in moves in the subsequent Fight Phase, helping you engage or trap the enemy.
  6. It’s harder to string models back in mid-sized units. Engagement range is so big that it’s much harder to block out your own models from it compared to mandatory basing in 10th Edition. You can choose to leave an individual model stationary as long as your other models can meet the criteria, but that’s a lot less flexibility than you often had in 10th.
  7. If you roll a charge, and you don’t fancy any of the targets you can reach, you can choose not to move. There’s no obligation to execute a charge after trying one.

The general trend here is for Charges to be smoother and a bit easier, and you’re going to get more big swingy high rolls happening simply because you no longer have to pre-select between an easy 5” and a potentially game-changing 11” option.

No Overwatch§

The other thing that will speed up the Charge Phase is that there is no Overwatch in it. That’s in the movement phase only now, so make sure you get your defensive shooting in then.

Heroic Intervention§

The Heroic Intervention Stratagem now happens at the end of the phase, and has been considerably buffed in 11th Edition. The standard 1CP version of this still only lets you counter-charge enemy units that have charged, but no longer has a 6” cap on it, so you can go for a big reach if it’s your only hope. You also resolve the charge same as above, so if multiple enemies have ended charges within range, you can tag both of them, or roll the dice to see if you hit a stretch goal, and select a consolation target if not.

There’s also now a 2CP version available to all armies that allows them to charge any units, but does cap their charge distance at 6”. This means you can no longer safely score by running up to a high threat melee unit and standing innocently on an objective in front of them, at least not if the opponent has space Command Points.

The Fight Phase§

The Fight Phase gets the biggest overhaul of any part of the turn in 11th Edition, shaking up both when and how units move, and who can fight. For those who felt combat movement got a bit too constrained in 10th Edition it’s good news - you have quite a bit more flexibility in how you position your units, and also some opportunities to create massive power plays with careful sequencing of your fights.

The Fight Phase has a start and end of phase step like all other phases in 11th Edition.

Pile-In§

The first big change in this phase is that all units now Pile In in a shared step as the first piece of action once start of phase rules are complete. Each unit that is either engaged or that made a charge move this turn is eligible to pile in. You start with the Active Player, who gets to make a pile-in move with all of their eligible units, after which the Opposing Player makes all of their pile-ins (but may find themselves constrained with what they can achieve).

A Pile-In move uses the rules template for a type of move, and lets each model in the unit move up to 3”, with some restrictions. Before moving, you select your Pile-In Targets, and when making a Pile-In, each model must end its move closer to and (if possible) engaged with the closest Pile-In Target. Which units you can select as targets varies depending on your current situation. If you are already engaged, you can only (and must) select all units you are currently engaged with as your targets. This can somewhat constrain your ability to slingshot into a unit you did not Charge, because you cannot select them as a target. With 2” engagement range, there’s still plenty of room to force foes into the fight, but you can’t just push off in a completely different direction.

If you are not currently engaged (rare in this step, but important shortly, stay tuned), you can instead select one or more units within 5” to be your Pile-In targets, and then move as above. Importantly, in this case there’s no longer a requirement that you must go for the closest target, so you can concentrate your efforts on a single target rather than getting pulled in different directions.

As with 10th Edition, models that are already in base contact with the enemy cannot make a Pile-In move. This means that although there’s no obligation to base foes with this any more, the Active Player will often want to do so when making their Pile-In moves in order to restrict their opponents options.

Eligibility to Fight§

Once all units have Piled-In, we move to the Fight Step, and need to determine which units can do so. All units that Charged are eligible to Fight, as are any that are engaged, and any that start this step stay eligible even if they later become unengaged. The latter is important; in previous editions, a unit that did not Charge could get pulled out of engagement as their foes suffered casualties, and not end up getting to Fight. Now, doing that doesn’t stop them getting their chance to contribute, and when they activate they will get to make an Overrun Fight, which gives them an additional Pile-In Move to get back into combat.

That doesn’t mean you cannot game the threat the foe poses by careful casualty allocation. Because units don’t get additional Pile-Ins by default, the trick now is to try and leave one model alive, just within 2” of a single model in the enemy squad, at which point they won’t get to Pile-In, and only that single model will swing. Doesn’t help if it’s a Primarch or something, but it can be a lifesaver against a large unit that kills by volume.

Which models in a unit are eligible to make attacks with their weapons is also different in 11th Edition. This is now solely determined by the 2” engagement distance - if you’re within that, you can swing, if you aren’t, tough luck. This still lets multiple ranks of models in a large unit fight, as 2” is a reasonably big distance, but with units that have really large bases, you can’t base one enemy model with one base, then have the rest of the unit fight through that.

What happens if you’re eligible to Fight, but aren’t engaged with any enemies? As mentioned above, this is handled by Overrun Fights.

Overrun Fight§

When a unit is selected to Fight, if they are either not currently Engaged or if they started the Fight Step unengaged, and are now engaged (usually because another unit has done what we’re about to talk about to them, or because of Consolidation later), they get to make an Overrun Fight. When doing this, the unit makes an additional Pile-In move prior to making attacks, giving them extra movement to tag their foes.

This can allow for some incredibly powerful Fight Phases for melee armies, because if they charge two alpha threats into a single target, and the first one swings and wipes them, the second unit gets to make an additional pile-in to reach new enemies and inflict carnage. Abilities that provide 6” Pile-Ins amplify this even more, and Berzerker Warband is going to do some absolutely unholy (or from Khorne’s point of view, extremely holy) things to unprepared players till they adjust to playing around this.

Selecting Units to Fight§

In the Fight Step, players take turns selecting units to Fight, starting with the Active player, and at any time if a player has no eligible units, they have to pass. First, all units with Fights First are selected (which includes those that Charged). If only one player has units with Fights First, they get to select all of these one after the other - the opponent can only pass.

After all Fight First units have been selected, other units get their chance. Importantly, the alternation continues straight onwards from Fight First units, so if one player selects the final Fight First unit, their opponent gets to pick the first “normal” fighter. If, at any point, a unit that has Fight First becomes eligible and hasn’t fought yet, then the step reverts to resolving Fight First units until all of those have run out again.

The most important change here is that the Active player chooses the first Fight First unit, rather than it being the Opposing player. Charging a powerful Fight First unit is no longer a death sentence, and the Fight First ability is correspondingly a lot less good. Fight First has been hugely warping in high-level play all through 10th Edition, and this makes it much easier to handle, particularly if a player only has one unit with access to it.

It does also mean there’s a route for the Active Player to pick the first normal Fight if they desperately need to. If you don’t charge with any units and thus don’t have anyone with Fight First, then your first selection will be a normal unit, which you can use to bail them out of a tight spot (at a cost of starting other Fights).

Making Attacks§

When units are selected to Fight (either a regular or Overrun Fight), their models get to make attacks with their melee weapons against an enemy unit they’re engaged with. This works the pretty same as making shooting attacks, so check out our Shooting Phase article for more details. The only major difference is that rather than splitting weapons between targets, each model can split its attacks as it chooses between the targets that it is engaged with, and that you are obliged to use all attacks you have available to you.

Consolidation§

Once both players pass, because no eligible units remain, there is a consolidation step, where both players get to move their units again. Starting with all of the Active Player’s units, each unit that was eligible to fight this phase gets to consolidate, one at a time.

Consolidation is a modal ability, and each unit that does so must use the relevant mode below.

If a unit is currently engaged, they use Ongoing Consolidation. They select every enemy unit they are engaged with as targets, then each model that is not currently in base-to-base contact can move up to 3”. Each must end their move closer to the nearest selected target, and engaged with them as possible (similar to Pile-In).

If a unit is not engaged, but is within 3” of an enemy unit (Note: we are wondering if this is supposed to be 5”, and are hoping an FAQ will clarify), then they select Engaging Consolidation, and must select one or more of those enemy units within 3” as targets. They then move models up to 3”, which must follow the same conditions as above (and in addition, the unit must engage all the selected targets). If any of the targets reached have not yet been selected to Fight this phase, they immediately become eligible and then Fight (one at a time, if there’s more than one). This means that more Fights, including Overrun Fights, can be triggered during this step.

There’s an interesting nuance here that if the Active Player pulls new Opposing Player units into battle at this point, those will later get to Consolidate themselves (because the Opposing Player hasn’t had their part of the Consolidation step yet), but if the Opposing Player does so in their part of the step, it’s too late for the Active Player’s units to Consolidate (there’s no circuit breaker to go back to an earlier part of the step as for Fight First).

The final Consolidation mode can be selected if neither of the above applies, but the unit is within 3” of an Objective. In this case, they can use Objective Consolidation, and move each model up to 3”. Each model that moves must end the move either within range of the Objective (so, within it if it’s a terrain feature), or closer to it if that’s not possible. This does mean that if you’re using Terrain Objectives (the default for 11th Edition) and are already on the terrain, you can freely move 3” on it as long as you don’t leave the terrain. With 6” Consolidation effects, this can be quite powerful for scooting behind a wall, or adding some vital distance to try and get Hidden online.

Wrap Up§

That’s it for the Charge and Fight Phases. If you want to read more about the rules, go check out our other deep dives from the links up top, or if you’re interested in models, missions or our overall opinions on 11th Edition, head back to the site’s front page, and you’ll find it all there.

articles.title: Rules Deep Dive: Terrain and Objectives
articles.html:
This article is our deep dive on Terrain and Objectives in 11th Edition, focusing on what’s changed in the new Edition.

This piece is aimed primarily at existing players, and we’re going to focus on what’s changed rather than digging into the full mechanics of the phase. If you’re a brand new player looking to dive into 11th Edition, we recommend you go and take a look at our main review, as that provides a full outline of how Warhammer 40,000 works on the tabletop!

As a note - for our review, we have been provided with access to the extended digital rules that will be made generally available in the Warhammer 40,000 app. This means that in a few places, we may talk about a rule or specific nuance that hasn’t been covered in previews or things you’ve seen elsewhere. We’ll try to flag the important ones!

Huge thanks to Games Workshop for providing us with review copies of all the material covered today. Please note that any digital material we refer to is subject to change until it is made available on Warhammer Community or the Warhammer 40k App. For this article, some images were also prepared using a provided review copy of the new Terrain Areas set.

Other Articles§

If this isn’t the part of the rules you were looking for, or you’re wondering what to look at next, check out the links to all our deep dive articles below:

  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Concepts
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Command Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Moving and the Movement Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Attacks and the Shooting Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: The Charge and Fight Phases
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Terrain and Objectives
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Special Unit Types
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Stratagems and Abilities

Terrain§

Terrain was one of the bits of 10th Edition that never really quite landed. 11th gives it a full overhaul, and also centres it even more in battles because key terrain areas on the table now take on the role of Objectives. Terrain can also protect models by providing Cover, blocking Visibility for shooting, or by letting light forces hunker down with the new Hidden rule.

Terrain is a particularly important topic, with tonnes of detail to go into. As well as this initial deep dive, the Ruleshammer team are hard at work preparing a full guide to terrain in 11th Edition, featuring loads of helpful diagrams to help you get to grips with the new rules.

Terrain Features and Terrain Areas§

When placing terrain on the battlefield, there are two components:

  • Terrain Feature: The actual, physical piece of terrain.
  • Terrain Area: a mat, template (such as from the new terrain areas pack GW have released) or agreed area that terrain features sit within.

The boundaries of the Terrain Area determine whether models are within the terrain for rules purposes, and also have a rules-based impact on Visibility drawn across them. The physical Terrain Features can impact on how models move across terrain areas, and also affect “true” Visibility to models in or around the Terrain Area.

Terrain Objectives§

Warhammer 40K battles are often fought over Objectives, vital areas of the battlefield that need to be secured. In 11th Edition, rather than these being independent areas or locations, the default is that one or more Terrain Areas on the battlefield is designated as an Objective. We’ll talk about controlling them later on in the article, but the important thing to know up front when reading about the Terrain rules is that holding Objectives now generally means placing yourself within a Terrain Area.

Terrain Categories§

Terrain Features are split into three categories:

  • Exposed: ground-level features like craters that don’t affect movement or visibility at all.
  • Light: Small walls, piles of crates, minor decorative statues.
  • Dense: Large ruins, buildings, big Munitorum Containers.

All Models can move through Light and Exposed terrain freely, whatever the size of the physical Feature. For Dense Terrain Features, INFANTRY, SWARMS and BEASTS can move through it freely, but other models must move around it, excluding parts of it that are <2” tall, which can also be moved through freely.

All Dense terrain also has the Solid rule. This has impacts on Visibility, and for movement means that models cannot end their move with any part of the model poking out through an enclosed gap in the terrain (e.g. a window) that’s <3” from the ground.

A Terrain Area can have multiple Terrain Features of different categories placed on it, and indeed this is a feature of the terrain layouts that Games Workshop have included to go with Chapter Approved. Sometimes this means lighter forces can dash through the centre of an area, while tanks and monstrosities have to burn some movement to wiggle around. You can see this in the image above - on the left, we have an area containing a single Dense feature, and on the right, one with a single Light feature. The middle area contains three features: two Light (the pipes) and one Dense (the large statue). All three of these setups are part of the Chapter Approved layouts.

Light and Dense Terrain Features also bequeath rules on Terrain Areas that they are within. Any area containing a Feature with either of these categories becomes Obscuring (impacting visibility through it) and makes the Area eligible for light forces to be Hidden within it. We’ll go through what all that means in this article.

Cover§

One of the key things that terrain provides is Cover, which inflicts a -1BS on ranged attacks targeting units.

A unit has cover against an attacking model if one of the following applies to every model within it:

  • The model has the INFANTRY/SWARM/BEAST keyword and is within a Terrain Area
  • The model is not fully visible to the attacker because of either an intervening Terrain Feature, or a partially intervening Terrain Area thanks to the Obscuring rule.

A really important change from 10th Edition is that Cover is now determined per attacking model, rather than all or nothing for the whole unit.
