If half of an attacking unit has full Visibility on a target, and half does not, then you split those two groups up and resolve their attacks separately, half with -1BS and half without. It also means that you have to think a bit more carefully about positioning your units defensively, because even one model fully exposed in the open takes Cover away from your entire unit, whereas in 10th you could do that, and still take saves on models with the Cover benefit (which in 10th was a reduction in AP).

This tends to mean that units will have Cover less often than they did in 10th Edition, and changing the penalty to affect the hit roll rather than AP means that attacks which do get through are more dangerous. AP-1 weaponry gets vastly better in 11th Edition, and mid-AP stuff with Sustained Hits (which mitigates the impact of the penalty) is especially strong.

Hidden§

Hidden is one of the marquee new mechanics for 11th Edition, allowing INFANTRY, SWARM and BEAST units to sacrifice their shooting to conceal themselves in terrain areas.

Important Note: There are some changes to Hidden between what’s in the rule book and the updated version of the rules that’s going to be pushed to the app, specifically that areas with only Light features are eligible for Hidden and the addition of the Gone to Ground rule. If you are reading this and thinking it’s slightly different to what you’ve seen elsewhere, that might be why!

A model with one of the qualifying keywords is Hidden if it is within a Terrain Area that contains at least one Light or Dense terrain feature, and that model’s unit has not made any Ranged Attacks this turn or the previous turn (if there was one, so you can be Hidden at the start of the battle). While Hidden, a model is not Visible to enemy units that are outside of its Detection Range, which is normally 15”.

Detection Range can be modified by rules on Datasheets (plenty of the previewed Detachments on Warhammer Community interact with it), and can also be impacted by the core Gone to Ground rule. If a model is Hidden, their unit did not make any ranged attacks, and is not fully visible to the attacking model because of an intervening Dense/Solid terrain feature, then Detection range is reduced by 3” to 12”. Notably, the requirement to not make Ranged Attacks here is explicitly separate to that for Hidden. Some units have rules that allow them to make Ranged Attacks and stay Hidden, but in this case they will not get the additional Gone to Ground benefit.

Hidden is something completely new, and is going to be crucial to the dynamics of 11th Edition, as it’s now your premier way to try and hold objectives safely. You are going to see a lot of Infantry nervously stepping a few models onto the side of a Terrain Area furthest from the opponent, ideally partially behind a Solid wall to drop the Detection Range to 12”. This can be pretty effective and safe - if the other models stay behind the terrain, they’ll not be Visible because of the Obscuring rule, and 12” is a pretty short distance for foes to have to reach, at least in the early turns of a game. From our experiments with the new edition, we’ve found that it’s especially good for Infantry units with Scout, as it generally broadens their options for where they can safely move out of the gate. As battle progresses, Hidden generally becomes harder to use, though can still have some crucial impacts.

Exactly how much this matters is something we’re going to discover as people play a lot more games, but we’ll say that the Gone to Ground rule feels like it makes a big difference compared to our initial impressions. 3” may not sound like a big change, but on the Chapter Approved layouts there’s plenty of positions where 15” will let you shoot from one Terrain Objective to pretty much the whole of another with impunity, and 12” won’t. It’s also exceptionally good for units like Space Marine Infiltrators that prevent foes from arriving from Reinforcements within 12”, as that can make them really hard to get a full shooting swing on. Units that can improve it further also love this, as you can get it as low as 9” before hitting the modifier cap, at which point you’re a real pain to pry out. We do still worry a bit that against optimised armies full of fast shooting hulls it doesn’t do enough to offset the changes to Obscuring that 11th brings, but it’s certainly felt like it’s adding some new dynamics in our test games.

Also, if you’re planning on using Overwatch with a unit, don’t forget that it will turn their ability to be Hidden off!

Obscuring§

Terrain Areas that contain Light or Dense features are Obscuring. This means that if neither the firing model or the target is within the Terrain Area, then it blocks visibility, thus allowing units to fully protect themselves behind an Area as long as their foe can’t step foot in it.

We’ve had rules like this in the last two Editions, but 11th does make a big change from 10th by requiring a firing model to only be within a terrain area to ignore it, rather than wholly within. The size and shapes of the official Terrain pack essentially means this has to be how things work, but wow does this open tables right up to large shooting pieces, especially if they can give themselves Assault somehow. This is probably our biggest structural concern with 11th Edition, full stop, particularly because a lot of the maps (24/45, for those counting) have a single giant 10”x10” ruin in the middle of the board, so if you can touch that it transforms your sightlines onto the table. The way other features are placed on the maps does try to mitigate this, and there are some very obvious “stage your forces here” positions, but I’m definitely not looking forward to experiencing my first Mont’ka full send in 11th. I expect to see an increased use of sacrificial chaff leveraging their 2” engagement range just to stop foes getting part of their model into Areas that provide key sightlines.

Climbing Terrain§

Many Terrain Features have upper floors or raised portions that models can be placed on. As long as a unit has the INFANTRY/SWARM/BEAST/FLY or MONSTER keywords, they can end a move on an upper floor as long as they fit stably, and their base isn’t overhanging the edge of whatever they’re sitting on. Monsters being able to do this is new for 11th Edition, and while most of them are going to be too big to fit on a lot of Features, if you’re playing on a table with a Skyshield Landing Pad and want your Carnifex to go out like King Kong, you can now live that dream.

11th also codifies how models should climb to upper floors. If you are within 0.5” horizontally of a Terrain Feature, you can move vertically straight up and down to climb it. Simple.

Plunging Fire§

Going to upper floors can expose your models, but has advantages. If a model is on a section of a terrain feature that is >3” tall, and is firing at a visible target with one or more models at ground level, their shooting attacks get +1BS, neatly cancelling out the benefits of cover (or providing a boost in lethality against targets in the open).

Getting models to 3” above ground level is a much easier ask than the 6” required in 10th Edition, so this will actually come up in more than 1% of games!

Objectives§

Controlling Objectives§

As discussed above, the default option for Objectives in 11th Edition is that you designate Terrain Areas on the Battlefield as them. A model is within range of a Terrain Objective if they are (unsurprisingly) within the Terrain Area, and at the end of each phase and the end of the turn, control of an Objective is determined by which player has the highest Level of Control over it. Level of Control is calculated by adding up the OC of all models that are within range of the objective, obviously accounting for any modifiers or things that remove OC like Battle-shock.

Some rules also check whether a specific unit is controlling an objective. This is the case if a unit has a model with at least OC1 within range of an objective that the unit’s owner controls (so interestingly, an OC0 unit can never be affected by such rules, even if it’s alongside a unit with OC1 that is controlling an objective).

Objective Secured§

Some rules allow an Objective to be Secured, which means it remains under one player’s control until their opponent has a higher Level of Control on it at the end of any phase. These rules existed in 10th Edition, but were written out separately each time, so this is a nice bit of streamlining. It’s also a very important rule in 11th Edition because of how much more dangerous holding objectives (sometimes even your home objective) can be, even with Hidden. If you can secure an objective, then if your opponent can’t reach out and grab it it stays yours, even if they blast you to smithereens.

Types of Objective§

11th Edition’s Chapter Approved missions introduce three different designations of Objectives, which incorporate some unofficial 10th Edition lingo into the actual rules. Objectives in Chapter Approved are either:

  • Home Objectives: Applied to a single objective in each player’s deployment zone.
  • Expansion Objectives: Applied to two objectives that are in no-man’s land, but closer to one player’s deployment zone, making it safer for them to move out and capture it.
  • Central Objectives: Applied to either a single central objective (on 30/45 maps) or two separate objectives slightly offset from the centre (the other 15).

Each official layout clearly labels which objectives are which.

Many Primary and Secondary Missions specify which type of objective you need to control for different conditions, with the greatest rewards often attached to the Central Objective(s) because of the higher risk of controlling them.

Non-Terrain Objectives§

In the appendix of the rulebook, there’s a rule for how to play with Objectives that are not Terrain Areas, which is to use a 40mm marker with a 3” control radius, exactly the same as in 10th Edition. This is clearly just there as a fallback - it’s not the intended way to play 11th Edition.

Wrap Up§

That’s it for the Terrain and Objectives. If you want to read more about the rules, go check out our other deep dives from the links up top, or if you’re interested in models, missions or our overall opinions on 11th Edition, head back to the site’s front page, and you’ll find it all there.

articles.title: Rules Deep Dive: Special Unit Types
articles.html:
This article is our deep dive on how some special types of Unit work in 11th Edition, covering VEHICLEs, MONSTERs and Leaders. As with all of our Deep Dives, we’re focusing on what’s changed in the new Edition.

This piece is aimed primarily at existing players, and we’re going to focus on what’s changed rather than digging into the full mechanics of the phase. If you’re a brand new player looking to dive into 11th Edition, we recommend you go and take a look at our main review, as that provides a full outline of how Warhammer 40,000 works on the tabletop!

As a note - for our review, we have been provided with access to the extended digital rules that will be made generally available in the Warhammer 40,000 app. This means that in a few places, we may talk about a rule or specific nuance that hasn’t been covered in previews or things you’ve seen elsewhere. We’ll try to flag the important ones!

Huge thanks to Games Workshop for providing us with review copies of all the material covered today. Please note that any digital material we refer to is subject to change until it is made available on Warhammer Community or the Warhammer 40k App.

Other Articles§

If this isn’t the part of the rules you were looking for, or you’re wondering what to look at next, check out the links to all our deep dive articles below:

  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Concepts
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Command Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Moving and the Movement Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Attacks and the Shooting Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: The Charge and Fight Phases
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Terrain and Objectives
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Special Unit Types
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Stratagems and Abilities

Leaders and Attached Units§

Some models, mostly Characters, have either the Leader or Support ability, which allows them to join other units to create a single Attached Unit. These operate as a single unit on the Battlefield, usually becoming more than the sum of their parts.

Leaders and Support§

In 11th Edition, there are two abilities that allow a unit to join other units, Leader and Support. Each works in pretty much the same way - such a unit will specify which units it can lead, and during the Muster Armies step of the battle, you can attach them to those units.

Why are there two different rules that do the same thing? This serves two purposes:

  1. It centralises rules that existed in 10th Edition allowing two Characters to join the same unit. Now, each unit can be joined by up to one Leader and up to one Support unit. As an example from the Armageddon box, the mighty Warboss is a Leader, while the Bannernob is Support, so if you want your Waaaagh’s leader and a fancy flag in the same unit, you can have that.
  2. Support units must join a unit during the Muster Armies step; they cannot choose to wander around the table solo. This is a slightly surprising change (and only appears in the extended App rules), and we suspect this is to close off a gap in 10th where Support-style Characters were often among the cheapest things in each Codex, so you saw them taken solo to sit on an objective rather than fulfil their intended buff role.

We won’t know what the full impact of this is till we see which Characters get switched to be Support, but directionally its very likely to broaden the number of double attachment options; even just taking the Datasheets in the Armageddon box, Orks now have vastly more options to do so than in 10th!

Attachments Happen During Army Construction§

Eagle-eyed readers may have spotted another change above - Attaching a Leader and/or Support unit to another unit now happens during Muster Armies, not the pre-game Battle Formations step. This simplifies list building a bit, but for my money is a little bit of a shame; 95% of the time when building a list in 10th the Attachments were fixed, but I quite liked the toolbox flexibility of being able to mix things up. I guess it will make Competitive Innovations easier to write though, because I won’t have to speculate in those 5% edge cases.

Attachment is Permanent§

In 10th Edition, although Attached Units operated as a single force, it was still possible to destroy separate parts of them. Attached Units are still divided into the Leaders/Support models and Bodyguard models, which are those from the units that were joined, but if you run out of Bodyguard Models, or a Leader gets iced via Precision, no unit has been Destroyed for rules purposes, and the unit is still an Attached Unit.

This has three main impacts:

  • If your opponent needs to destroy a unit to fulfil a mission, they have to take out every last model, and units containing a Bodyguard, Leader and Support will no longer give up three kills for missions that care about the number.
  • As long as they are alive, Leader/Support models in an Attached are always “leading a unit” for the purpose of abilities they have which care about this. A Librarian no longer forgets how to provide an Invulnerable Save after running out of Bodyguard models (though this effect will stop if the Librarian dies - see below).
  • Effects that revive models can put a Bodyguard back together even if every Bodyguard model got wiped out. Be assured that as a Necron player I am salivating at the prospect of this.

Taken together with the timing changes, this means that if you’re building a big doomstack for your army, you are fully locked-in to that choice, but the unit you get will hold onto its benefits longer, and is less of a scoring liability in some missions.

Abilities in Attached Units§

One of the big reasons to form Attached units is to let Leaders and Bodyguards benefit from one-another’s abilities. Most Leaders provide a buff to their units, and some units have abilities providing re-rolls that can soup up the damage output of more martial Leaders.

When either part of a unit has an ability, all models in it benefit as long as at least one model from the Datasheet that provides the ability is still alive. This means that although an Attached unit is now a permanent fixture, you can still strip away powerful Leader effects by using Precision and the like.

An exception to this is abilities (such as those provided by Wargear) that only affect a specified bearer - these obviously still only affect the specific model, and stop working if that model is destroyed.

Note: One of the things we’re not totally sure about is how this works with some Core Abilities, such as Feel No Pain and Infiltrators*, where only one Datasheet in the Attached Unit has them. Based on the Attached units rules, it looks like these might propagate automatically, but the wording in the Core Abilities suggests that it still cares about which specific models have the ability. We’re hoping for a clarification on this, as especially for Feel No Pain, it seems unlikely the intention is that every Leader with one can now provide that as an extra unit-wide bonus!*

Keywords In Attached Units§

Different Datasheets that form an Attached unit may have different Keywords. When an attached unit forms, each model still only has the Keywords provided by its own Datasheet, but the Attached Unit has all the Keywords. This can make the whole unit eligible for powerful buffs from Stratagems and Detachment Rules, but also makes them vulnerable to rules like Anti.

One thing that’s not totally clear in the rulebook is whether effects that require a certain Keyword affect or can target an Attached unit where every model with that Keyword has been Destroyed. The answer to this is provided in the extended App rules - they cannot, so if you want to use a buff for INTERCESSORS on an Attached Unit of five Intercessors and a Captain, at least one Intercessor needs to be alive. For the smart-ass at the back raising their hand to ask if that creates weird issues with timing on Anti- weapons; no, they thought ot that, and:

  1. Anti- is determined at target time.
  2. In 11th, models destroyed by attacks are not removed until the attacking unit has resolved all of its attacks.

Monsters & Vehicles§

All armies in Warhammer 40K can bring either mechanical war machines or terrifying monsters (or both) to bear, and these are designated by the VEHICLE and MONSTER keywords. These have some general rules that apply to them.

Moving Monsters & Vehicles§

Monsters and Vehicles have two differences in how they move around the Battlefield:

  • Normally you can move through friendly models, but Monsters/Vehicles cannot move through other models with these Keywords. They’re too big and would smash into each other!
  • Normally you cannot move through enemy models. When making a Normal or Advance move, Monsters and Vehicles can move through enemy models that do not share the Monster/Vehicle keyword.

The first part is the same as in 10th Edition, but the second is new, and makes it harder to fully trap and move-block big models with a line or circle of chaff. One of my least edifying games of 10th Edition involved my Avatar of Khaine being trapped against a wall by three consecutive 5-model units of Drukhari Wyches forming a little ring turn after turn. In 11th, the Avatar could simply walk out and leave.

Frame§

Some Monster/Vehicle models don’t have bases, or protrude considerably beyond them. In this case, they are given the FRAME keyword, which indicates that when placing and measuring distances to the model, all parts of the model are used (the closest, for distance), not just the base. This codifies “measure to hull” into an actual Keyword and rule, which is a big relief all-round.

Frame also explains how you pivot a model without a base - you do so around the central point of the model.

Towering and Plunging Fire§

Knight Abominant - Norman

Some especially large units have the TOWERING keyword, looming large across the Battlefield. In 10th Edition, this modified how they could see and be seen through terrain, but that’s no longer the case in 11th.

Instead, any model with TOWERING that is making ranged attacks against visible foes on ground level that are within 12” automatically benefits from the Plunging Fire rule, getting +1 to their BS. This makes aggressive shooting plays by Imperial and Chaos Knight armies especially terrifying to foes!

Shooting and Engaged Monsters/Vehicles§

Monsters and Vehicles are so large that the enemy can safely shoot at them even when their forces are in Engagement Range. They suffer a -1 penalty to hit while doing so, unless they're engaged with it and shooting Close-Quarters weapons.

Aircraft§

Some factions bring close air support to the battlefield, raking the battlefield with firepower from planes or winged creatures. These have always been a bit awkward, rules-wise, and 11th Edition overhauls them yet again.

Moving Aircraft§

In 11th Edition, Aircraft do not have a Movement Characteristic, and can only ever perform an Ingress Move. At the end of your opponent’s turn, each Aircraft unit you control that’s on the Battlefield must be placed into Strategic Reserves, ready to Ingress again on the following turn. Essentially, Aircraft now make true strafing runs, appearing, shooting, and then vanishing again after the enemy has had a window to fire back or try and smash them from the sky. Aircraft must also start the battle in Strategic Reserves (and do use up your points allocation for reserves), so can only start doing this from the second Battle Round onwards unless they have an ability that lets them do otherwise.

This is, on balance, probably slightly better than their movement situation in 10th, where they still had to make an initial appearance from reserves on turn 2, but were then locked-in to making at least a 20” move straight ahead from that position, often ending up somewhere awkward. They could fly off the board, but then you’d lose a whole turn of shooting with them!

Aircraft also cannot ever make a Charge Move.

Shooting with Aircraft§

Aircraft ignore the Plunging Fire rule in both directions, but otherwise shoot normally. This is a notable change from 10th Edition - Aircraft used to ignore the Obscuring rule both when shooting or being shot at. This change generally makes aircraft a bit less appealing, especially as unless they have Deep Strike, they will always be deploying around the edges of the board, which will give them restricted sight lines on many maps. Bluntly, Aircraft just aren’t very good in 10th Edition, and the times they showed up were usually as tech pieces on specific terrain packs (e.g. UKTC) where being able to shoot into specific staging spots through terrain was high value. Without that, they’ll need to be pretty cheap to be worth taking.

Interacting with Aircraft§

Because an Aircraft’s position on the battlefield is a fairly abstract representation of the plane zipping past, they have limited battlefield impact on units without FLY. Enemy models can always move through Aircraft, and enemy models without FLY can largely ignore them for the purposes of rules that require picking and moving towards nearby enemy units (e.g. Surge Moves). Aircraft do still have an Engagement Range that models cannot normally end moves within.

Only models with FLY can interact with Aircraft in the Charge/Fight Phases as well. Only Units with FLY can select Aircraft as Charge Targets, and only they can make melee attacks at Aircraft.

This does plausibly create some slightly weird interactions against non-FLY units, as the Engagement Range around an Aircraft can be huge, and potentially impact on nearby enemies being able to successfully charge targets sheltering near them. This probably only ever arises for Aircraft that can Deep Strike, as they’re the only ones that will end up in places where precision melee placement will really matter, but I suspect at some point the restrictions here will change to let non-FLY units end a Charge Move within engagement of an Aircraft, even if they didn’t select it as a Charge Target.

Wrap Up§

That’s it for special Unit types. If you want to read more about the rules, go check out our other deep dives from the links up top, or if you’re interested in models, missions or our overall opinions on 11th Edition, head back to the site’s front page, and you’ll find it all there.

articles.title: Rules Deep Dive: Core Stratagems and Abilities
articles.html:
This article is our deep dive on the core abilities and stratagems in 11th Edition, focusing on what’s changed in the new Edition.

This piece is aimed primarily at existing players, and we’re going to focus on what’s changed rather than digging into the full mechanics of the phase. If you’re a brand new player looking to dive into 11th Edition, we recommend you go and take a look at our main review, as that provides a full outline of how Warhammer 40,000 works on the tabletop!

As a note - for our review, we have been provided with access to the extended digital rules that will be made generally available in the Warhammer 40,000 app. This means that in a few places, we may talk about a rule or specific nuance that hasn’t been covered in previews or things you’ve seen elsewhere. We’ll try to flag the important ones!

Huge thanks to Games Workshop for providing us with review copies of all the material covered today. Please note that any digital material we refer to is subject to change until it is made available on Warhammer Community or the Warhammer 40k App.

Other Articles§

If this isn’t the part of the rules you were looking for, or you’re wondering what to look at next, check out the links to all our deep dive articles below:

  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Concepts
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Command Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Moving and the Movement Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Attacks and the Shooting Phase
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: The Charge and Fight Phases
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Terrain and Objectives
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Special Unit Types
  • 11th Edition 40k Rules Deep Dive: Core Stratagems and Abilities

Stratagems§

Stratagems as a concept haven’t changed that much. You still spend CP for effects and can’t use the same stratagem more than once per phase.

One Stratagem Per Phase§

A big change here is you cannot use multiple stratagems on the same units each phase, meaning you can’t do stuff like activate Heroic Intervention, then re-roll the Charge.

This one is going to trip people up a lot early on. It’s clearly here to stop some of the apocalyptic combos you could assemble with multiple buffs before, and it’s basically a good change, but until people assimilate it, they’re going to forget that Command re-roll is a Stratagem. This does also change the value of some Stratagems depending on timing - an Advance/Charge Stratagem you activate in the Movement Phase is now much better than one activated in the Charge Phase, because with the latter if you then flub the charge you’re invested in, you’re out of luck! It also raises the value of non-Stratagem abilities providing Charge re-rolls.

Multiple Stratagem Costs§

Some stratagems now have multiple modes, which let you spend more CP for better effects. Heroic Intervention is an example of this from the core stratagems, allowing you to countercharge into an aggressive foe for 1CP, or go on the offensive against someone cowering too close for 2CP.

Unchanged Stratagems§

The following Stratagems are unchanged:

  • Epic Challenge: Allows one of your Characters to try and assassinate an enemy Character in melee via Precision (though how Precision works has changed, see below).
  • Insane Bravery: Allows a unit to automatically pass morale, once per game.
  • Counter Offensive: Allows a unit to interrupt the order of combat by temporarily gaining Fights First.
  • Grenades/Explosives: Allows a unit to inflict Mortal Wounds by hurling explosives.

The rules for the last one are unchanged but Grenades is now called Explosives to be more inclusive of bundles of dynamite and the like (though is still compatible with the existing Keyword).

Changed Stratagems§

There are a few changes:

  • Command Re-Reroll: Lets you re-roll a dice roll. You now re-roll just one die except when doing charges. This is a minor buff to all those 2d6 shot flamethrowers out there.
  • Crushing Impact: Lets your Monsters/Vehicles crash into the foe for Mortal Wounds. This is the new Tank Shock. Monsters can do it now and on 1’s you do a mortal to yourself.
  • Rapid Ingress: With the codification of Ingress Moves to the core rules, this now just lets you make one of those at the end of your opponent’s Movement Phase. It also can no longer target Aircraft and with the new rules for those, you probably didn’t wanna do that anyway.
  • Fire Overwatch: Allows you to shoot at the end of your Opponent's Movement Phase. Similarly to Rapid Ingress, this gets an integration with a new core rule called Snap Shooting. When Snap Shooting, you resolve your shooting attacks as normal, except that you only hit on 6s, and cannot re-roll them. This makes non-Torrent options quite a bit weaker in Overwatch, as most strong Overwatch units relied on re-rolls to get a good number of hits. Weapons with Sustained Hits are your other good option to get a decent number of hits in. The timing of Overwatch has also been changed to the end of the Movement Phase, and there's no requirement to target an enemy that moved. This shakes up the dynamics a bit - you can no longer freeze all nearby enemies with a powerful Overwatch threat, as they can run and hide behind a wall, but it means that enemies that do stay still within sight (which can include enemy Monsters/Vehicles in combat) are potential targets. The unit firing Overwatch has to be unengaged, so your Monsters/Vehicles in combat still can't do it.
  • Smokescreen: Lets a unit gain cover via triggering smoke launchers or grenades. With the new Cover rules, this now just gives you that. The thing is though, now the unit targeted by the Stratagem provides cover to the stuff behind the unit which is cool.
  • Heroic Intervention: Allows you to perform a counter-charge at the end of the enemy's Charge Phase. This now triggers at the end of the Charge Phase, and improves in a number of ways. When you use it, you make a Charge Roll, and with the standard, 1CP version you can then select any number of enemy units that Charged within 12” as a Target. If you pay an extra CP, you instead cap your Charge roll at 6”, but can now charge anything within 6”. With Overwatch moving to movement phase only, this is your counterplay in the charge phase. It opens up a lot of options for armies that suffer from being move-blocked too, and makes it harder to steal objectives with pure movement, as opponents can use this to push back.
  • Go To Ground: This one’s gone full stop. There is a rule of the same name now in the game but it’s part of the Hidden rule, giving you an extra -3” to detection range if every model is obscured by terrain

Core Abilities§

What’s the Same§

The following Core Abilities are unchanged from last edition:

  • Anti: Weapons that are especially good against certain targets.
  • Assault: Can be shot after advancing.
  • Close-quarters/pistol: Can be shot in engagement (Close-quarters is a new identical ability that will gradually replace Pistol).
  • Lance: +1 to Wound when charging.
  • One-Shot: Can be fired once per game.
  • Sustained: Extra hits on a critical hit.
  • Twin-Linked: Re-roll wounds.
  • Rapid Fire: Extra shots within half range.
  • Torrent: Hits automatically.
  • Feel No Pain: A chance to ignore damage each time you suffer it.
  • Firing Deck: An ability for transports allowing models inside to effectively shoot out of it.
  • Ignore Cover: Does what it says on the tin.

There’s a pretty clear asterisk on that last one that now Ignores Cover affects your hit roll instead of your opponent’s save, but it still just ignores that mechanic.

What’s Different§

There are a few smaller changes that tighten things up or expand the scope of what things can affect.

  • Blast: Extra shots targeting large units. Now has the X qualifier after, giving you that many shots per models in the unit.
  • Lethal Hits: Automatic wounds on a critical hit. Is now optional, letting you save for a critical wound roll if you want to fish for Devastating wounds.
  • Devastating Wounds: Deals Mortal Wounds instead of normal damage on a critical wound. Is mostly the same but has been rewritten to work like the rebalanced version of it at the end of 10th. They also all happen as a batch at the end of resolving your attacks which is a bit cleaner than before.
  • Infiltrators (set up in no-man's land) and Deep Strike (Ingress anywhere on the Battlefield) have been rewritten to use 8” for their measurements, which puts them in line with the new charge rules.
  • Hazardous simply makes you make a Hazard roll after using the weapon which makes sense.
  • Lone Operative: Prevents you being targeted within a certain distance, 12" by default. Also gets the X qualifier, telling you how many inches away it has to be before it can be targeted.
  • Melta: Extra damage in half range. Now changes the damage characteristic of the attack by the value, and also worth noting that the change in ordering to modifiers means that this no longer bypasses half damage or set-to-zero effects.
  • Indirect Fire Allows a weapon to be used for Indirect Shooting.
  • Deadly Demise: A chance for a unit to blow up when Destroyed. For Transports, now happens after any units Disembark and it was always weird that this wasn’t the case.
  • Extra Attacks: Additional melee weapons used in addition to others. The Attacks value can now be modified! There are a small subset of units like Bloodcrushers with Skarbrand that get a decent bump from this. A few units like Kill Rigs have a great time in the Ork Waaagh as well.
  • Stealth: Now provides you with Cover.

Now for the bigger changes:

  • Super-heavy Walker: brings the Knight faction rule into the core rules. Letting you move these models through terrain more than 4” tall, risking Battle-shock on a 1.
  • Fight First: Lets a unit Fight before units without this ability. Has no wording change, but plays very differently because of the updates to the Fight Phase. This means you no longer fight before your opponent, making this slightly less valuable.
  • Precision: Attacks that can attempt to snipe or assassinate Characters in Attached Units. Now works by changing the allocation order at the start of the Allocate Attacks step, putting one Character group at the start of the order for the batch of attacks with Precision. This means that you’re committed to it for the whole batch of attacks, but it also means that if you’re using one of the abilities that hands it to a whole unit (e.g. the Stratagem in Lions of the Emperor) it suddenly becomes exceptionally deadly, because the Character you’re hitting is guaranteed to eat the worst saving throws. It does also mean that a given group of attacks can only ever kill one Character, as each Character is always in a separate allocation group.
  • Heavy: Improved accuracy when stationary or moving slowly. This one’s a bit of a mixed bag. On the upside, it still applies to units where no model has moved >3”, so small repositioning to line up shots is possible. On the downside, if you are engaged, it does not apply, which means vehicles firing out of combat can no longer use it to mitigate the hit penalty for that.
  • Cleave X: is new, and is melee Blast, only applies if you put all your attacks into a single target unit. This is a fantastic addition to the toolbox of abilities and something we’ve wanted to see for a while.
  • Hover: is a new ability that lets you ignore the -2” payment to use FLY.
  • Psychic: represents weapons charged with psychic energy. This is no longer just a downside for your opponent to get ways to mitigate your rules! You can ignore all WS/BS/hit roll modifiers, which is absolute fire with the new Cover rules.
  • Scouts: Provides a pre-game move. There are two re-works here (and also now happens during resolve pre-battle abilities). The bad news for Space Marine fans - it no longer works if you are deployed outside of your deployment zone. However, it has a cool new option that if a unit with Scouts starts in Strategic Reserves, then at the start of the first Battle Round it can set up anywhere within your Deployment Zone. This can provide some powerful flexibility on scoring/utility units.
  • Surge Moves: Allow a unit to move after being shot at, including into engagement. Now have their own little section and can no longer be done if you already moved this phase as well as forcing you to move as close to the target as possible.
  • Plunging Fire: Improved accuracy from high ground. Now triggers at 3” and gives you +1 BS (countering cover, effectively). The move the 3” means it’ll now work with most of the terrain in the game and may actually come up during games.
  • Hunter X: A totally new effect hidden deep in the digital rules. Hunter weapons specify a Keyword, and can only target units with that Keyword. We have zero examples of this in the wild as of yet, so we’ll have to wait and see what they do with this one.
  • Heal X: Also covered in the digital rules, and seen on a previewed Stratagems for Chaos Daemons. Heal is a new rules effect that works like the current Necron Reanimation protocols. When you Heal a unit, you check if there are any models with missing Wounds, and recover one of those wounds if there are any. If there are not, you instead return one model to the unit with a single wound remaining. You then repeat this X times. If Heal specifies that it affects a single model, then only that model can recover wounds, and any excess are wasted.

Wrap Up§

That’s it for core abilities and Stratagems. If you want to read more about the rules, go check out our other deep dives from the links up top, or if you’re interested in models, missions or our overall opinions on 11th Edition, head back to the site’s front page, and you’ll find it all there.

articles.title: Ruleshammer: Shooting, Range, Line of Sight, and Visibility
articles.html:
Welcome back to Ruleshammer! In this series we take a detailed look at the rules of key games, but Warhammer 40k in particular. This week I’m going through a few of the subtleties for Shooting in 11th edition, looking in detail at Range, Line of Sight, and Visibility.

Shooting in Warhammer 40k 11 edition has a few changes and updates from previous editions that might surprise new and veteran players alike, let’s dive in.

SELECT TARGETS04.02§

The source of one of the most subtle changes in the edition happens here at step 2 of the attack sequence.

WHILE SHOOTING

Select one enemy unit to be the target of that weapon. Unless otherwise stated, each target must be:

    • Visible to the model that has that weapon (06.01).
    • Within range of that weapon.
    • Unengaged

Unlike previous editions, the model that is visible and the model that is in range of a specific weapon no longer need to be the same model. It’s a very subtle change, so much so that they already FAQed to confirm this is intended.

Q. Can a model be within range of a weapon if it is not visible?

A. Yes

Let's look at an example of this playing out.

What does this mean for Rapid Fire, Melta, etc?§

They continue to work as they have done for a while, but without being a slightly odd exception that works differently to how weapon range and visibility checks work. MELTA is concerned with how far away the unit is, not how far away a visible model in the target unit is.

This ability always takes the form [MELTA X]. Each time a model makes an attack with a [MELTA] weapon, if the target unit was within half range of that weapon in the Select Targets step, until the attacking unit’s attacks have been resolved, add X to that weapon’s D characteristic.

And so is RAPID FIRE.

This ability always takes the form [RAPID FIRE X]. Each time you gather attack dice for a [RAPID FIRE] weapon, add X additional attack dice if the target unit was within half range of that weapon in the Select Targets step.

Note also that this is checked at the “select targets step” so even if you have a unit with several weapons, and only some of them have the MELTA ability, if you resolve the none-MELTA weapons first that does not prevent the MELTA ability from triggering if the unit was within half range when you selected targets.

Line of Sight and Visibility Checks06.01§

EDIT: This section has been amended since publication based on feedback, there is some debate on how this section should be interpreted and I think it might require an FAQ.

One aspect I feel is currently left slightly unclear is how to check if a model is fully visible or not. We've been given a technical definition for a Line of Sight but not specifically told when to use it, other than to determine if models are purely visible or not.

Line of sight is used to determine visibility between models. For an observing model to have line of sight, it must be possible to draw an imaginary straight line, 1 mm wide, from any part of that model to any part of the model being observed. This line is the line of sight. While doing so, other models in the observing model’s unit and in the observed model’s unit are ignored.

Other models and units can be either visible or fully visible to the observing model, as shown opposite. Note that terrain applies additional rules to visibility (13.07).

The rules then go say that visible or fully visible is defined by the set of example images on page 25 of the Core Rules. We've mulled this over around the TTB water cooler and we're still not sure (or at least I'm not fully convinced). The text around the "fully visible" example image potentially implies that lines are intended to be imagined.

If every part of another model that is facing the observing model is visible to the observing model (so the only thing blocking visibility to any part of that other model is that model itself), that model is fully visible.

As some of us think that to determine if a part "is visible" you follow the rule as written above, imagining lines from the attacking model to every part of the target. In my opinion this doesn't fully line up with the example images but they could just be bad examples, and it also leaves the open question of if all of these lines need to be originate from one part of the attacking model, of if they can originate from many parts.

Hopefully we get an FAQ on this soon so that I can finally put this issue to rest.

Can Models block Visibility to other models?§

Technically it’s possible though very few models are completely opaque in this way, you can often shoot under most tanks even if they’re on wheels or tracks. My advice would generally be to presume such models won’t block Line of Sight and to check with your opponent if you think one should or currently is blocking a model from view.

Relatedly though whilst models in other units can make a model not fully visible, being not fully visible doesn’t do anything on its own. You need another rule triggered by being not fully visible such as the Benefit of Cover 13.08 rule provided by terrain features. There are some models that have rules like this, the Baneblade for instance can provide cover to models not fully visible due to its bulky frame. The smokescreen stratagem causes the model you use it on to provide cover also.

toc.title: What does this mean for Rapid Fire, Melta, etc?
Line of Sight and Visibility Checks
Can Models block Visibility to other models?

articles.title: Ruleshammer: Charge Phase
articles.html:
Welcome back to Ruleshammer! In this series we take a detailed look at the rules of key games, but Warhammer 40k in particular. This week we're looking at the charge phase!

The Charge Phase [11]§

Charging in 11th edition has some significant changes to be aware of, this guide covers the basics of the new charge phase rules but also aims to highlight areas that might cause confusion.The charge phase is broken up into 3 stages, though stage 11.02 represents the bulk of activity during the phase where units actually declare, attempt and make charges.

Start of the Charge Phase [11.01]§

This stage simply exists for “start of the charge phase rules” to be triggered. I’d expect to see more rules use this stage directly by name and number as the edition progresses but nothing much else has changed about it.

Declaring Charges [11.02]§

Here we start to see the big changes from previous editions. This step is split up into 3 distinct sub steps, but you go through each of them for every unit you’re charging with.

  1. Declare Charge: Select one friendly unit that has not declared a charge this phase and is eligible to declare a charge (see below). That unit declares a charge.

A unit is eligible to declare a charge if it is on the battlefield, unless otherwise stated. Here are some rules that prevent a unit from being eligible to declare a charge

    • It is not within 12" of one or more enemy units.
    • It is engaged.
    • It made an advance or fall-back move this turn.

It’s important to note this is not an exhaustive list, there are abilities and rules that prevent charging spread through the faction rules but another prominent one is the very last effect of the Emergency Disembark 18.05 move

AFTER MOVING: Your unit is battle-shocked and, until the end of the turn, it is not eligible to declare a charge.

So you still can’t purposefully try to get your own transports destroyed, such as by taking damage from their own hazardous weapons or by collateral damage from the Crushing Impact stratagem, to get a unit closer to the enemy for a charge.

Another key point is that charges no longer declare a specific target at this step, you need eligible targets but you don’t need to choose a specific one. Also it’s possible for a target to be eligible but not actually “chargeable”, usually due to terrain.

Moving through terrain works the same in the charge phase as it does in general. I’d suggest a read of the Ruleshammer Terrain Guide if you’re unsure on what models can move through, over, or on top of terrain.

Once you have a unit you want to charge with, the next step is making a charge roll.

2. Make Charge Roll: Make a charge roll by rolling 2D6: the result is the maximum distance for the charge move.

There’s not much to say about this other than reminding players that this roll is now BEFORE you select charge targets.

The Charge Move [11.04]§

Yes the rule number is correct, it goes 01, 02, 04, then 03. The charge move is the bread and butter of the phase and it has some quirks to be aware of. The first two elements are fairly straightforward, Charge Moves are moves only units that have declared a charge can make, and they follow the same movement rules as described in section 03. It gets a bit more subtle at the Before Moving section though.

BEFORE MOVING: Select one or more enemy units that are within 12” of your unit and within the maximum distance of your unit, until the end of this move, each of those enemy units is a charge target.

The key thing to notice here is that the target has to be within the distance you rolled! So if a unit is 10” away and you roll a 9” charge then you can’t select that unit, even though 9” would be sufficient distance to reach the 2” engagement range.

This rule can significantly change the outcome of a charge, especially when terrain is involved. Here's an example of a monster/vehicle attempting two different charges into a unit behind a wall.

The next step is probably one of the most confusing ones. I’ve seen a lot of suggestions online that you are required to get to base to base contact when charging. An idea I think supported by the charge distance requirement of selecting a target, this is NOT the case. Whilst targets must be within that distance there is no requirement to end in base to base contact when making a charge move. There are other requirements though.

WHILE MOVING

    • Each model must end its move closer to one or more charge targets.
    • Each model that can end its move within 1” of one or more charge targets must do so.
    • Each mode that can end its move engaged with one or more charge targets must do so.

As you can see, no requirements to end as close as possible or in base to base contact, which is important as models in base to base contact with enemy models can’t make Pile In moves in the fight phase!

AFTER MOVING:

    • Your unit must be engaged with all of the charge targets.
    • Your unit cannot be engaged with one or more enemy units that are not charge targets.
    • Until the end of the turn, each model in your unit has the Fights First ability (24.13).

One last thing to note is that actually making a charge move is optional, you can decide not to move. Be aware though that declaring a charge is not sufficient to be chosen to fight in the next phase, units need to either be engaged with an enemy unit or have made a charge move to be selected to fight.

End of the Charge Phase [11.03]§

Once you’ve completed all your charge declarations and charge moves the phase comes to a close. Any rules that trigger “at the end of the charge phase” are resolved here.

articles.title: Ruleshammer: Fight Phase
articles.html:
Welcome back to Ruleshammer! In this series we take a detailed look at the rules of key games, but Warhammer 40k in particular. This week I’m looking at some tricky bits of the Fight Phase.

The Fight phase has had much of the same treatment as the other phases, it’s gained specific start and end steps with numbers of their own and it’s had significant elements codified into specific moves and types of fight. This article focuses on some of the more nuanced changes and questions you might find yourself asking as you work your way through the phase.

PILE IN MOVES12.02§

In a significant change from previous editions, in 11th Pile In moves happen for all eligible units BEFORE any fights occur. Also activations for this step do NOT alternate between players, the active player makes all of their Pile In moves before their opponent makes theirs. Units can only make one Pile In move during this step.

What units are eligible?§

ELIGIBLE IF: It is the Fight phase and one or more of the following apply to your unit:

    • It is engaged.
    • It made a charge move this turn.
    • It was selected to make an overrun fight this phase (12.06).

This is pretty standard stuff, but the Overrun fight element of this rule does come out of the blue a bit, as Overrun fights aren't explained until later in the rules. At least in 11th we have handy rules numbers telling us where to go look for answers! I think the big question I've seen so far though is this.

What if a Unit becomes eligible during the Pile In step after I’ve moved my other units?§

This isn’t going to happen very often, and in the example below it’s something the Guard player almost definitely wants to avoid doing, but it can happen and I thought it worth exploring.

Whilst maybe a bit odd that the Immortals don't get to pile, the step is written without any swapping back to check for models that have become eligible during the step. They are eligible to fight but as only the Overrun fight type gets to make a Pile In Move they won't get to Pile In then either, they're not eligible to do an Overrun fight (read on for more detail on why that is).

PILE IN MOVES TRIGGERING OVERRUN FIGHTS12.06§

Overrun fights are a whole new concept in 11th edition, with one of the most situational secondary triggers I think I've ever seen in the game. Just thinking up an example for when it might occur took some thinking!

ELIGIBLE IF: Your unit is unengaged, or was unengaged at the start of the Fight step but became engaged during the Fight phase.

Take specific note that the second criteria requires a unit that is unengaged at the start of the Fight Step not the phase. This is why the Immortals in the previous diagram would not be eligible to make an Overrun Fight, they would start the step engaged. If things had played out differently though, there’s a version of events where they would get to make an Overrun fight.

Remember that an Overrun fight is still a fight type meaning that the unit needs to be eligible to be selected to fight in the first place. To be eligible to fight a unit must meet one or more of the following conditions.

    • It is engaged, or it was engaged at the start of this step.
    • It made a charge move this turn

That this is checked at the start of the step is actually a significant change from 10th edition, as previously you could actually stop a unit from from getting to fight by removing all the models engaged with it. In the scenario in the diagram above, if this was 10th edition the Heavy Weapons Team wouldn't have gotten to fight at all, but in 11th they not only get to fight to get to make an Overrun fight!

CONSOLIDATION12.07§

Consolidation like Pile In moves have been moved to a step of their own after all the Fights have been resolved, other than fights that consolidation can actually cause to happen that is. The consolidation move is pretty complex as it has several modes. The full move looks like this.

From Warhammer 40k 11th Edition - Pg42

Lets break down what these three modes really are fundamentally first.

Ongoing Consolidations - This mode is for units that are still engaged at the end of their fight, they represent models repositioning to continue the melee. If units are engaged at the start of this step they are required to choose this mode of consolidation.

Engaging Consolidations - This mode is fairly similar to consolidation moves of the past but slightly more restrictive still. Units that are unengaged but within 3” of any enemy units must select this mode and then end the move engaged with the units they selected when choosing it. If that unit or units hasn’t fought in the phase they get to do so now!

There’s some speculation that the distance for this mode is meant to be 5” rather than 3”; this would prevent the issue I’m above to cover with the final consolidation type.

Objective Consolidation - Selectable only if your unit doesn’t meet the criteria that require use of the first two modes, this mode allows a unit to consolidate towards and inside the area of an objective.

The issue though is that as written nothing prevents a unit from consolidating onto an objective that has enemy models outside of 3” on and ending that consolidation move within engagement range of that unit. We’re pretty sure here at TTB HQ that this isn’t intended to be allowed. Especially when Engaging Consolidation is the only mode that allows units to fight back when consolidated into.

The Tacoma FAQ did actually get released as this article was being written and whilst it didn't make any changes to the Engaging Consolidation distance it did rule that units that choose Objective Consolidation can not end that move engaged with enemy units. When this FAQ gets made part of the official rules we'll update this article to reflect that!

articles.title: Ruleshammer: Save Groups, Precision, and Feel No Pain
articles.html:
Welcome back to Ruleshammer! In this series we take a detailed look at the rules of key games, but Warhammer 40k in particular. This week we're looking at save groups, precision attacks, and feel no pain rolls!

Saving Groups05.03§

Saves in Warhammer 40k 11th edition are resolved in groups based on certain characteristics and keywords of the target unit. For most units there will be one group, but things get more complex if the unit has any variations in their Wound, Save, and Invulnerable Save characteristics or if the unit has any attached characters.

Create Groups: Divide all models in the target unit into the following groups, as many times as required:

    • One group for each CHARACTER model.
    • One group for all other models with the same W, Sv and InSv characteristics

Once you’ve identified your groups you need to allocate what order you’re going to resolve them in before you start making any saves. It’s not an entirely free choice though, some groups must be resolved before or after others based on a few key triggers.

Allocation Order: Declare the order in which those groups will have attacks allocated to them, applying all of the following:

    • If a non-CHARACTER group contains a model that has lost one or more wounds, that group must be first in the allocation order.
    • No CHARACTER group can be earlier in the allocation order than a non-CHARACTER group.
    • CHARACTER groups containing a model that has lost one or more wounds must be earlier in the allocation order than CHARACTER groups containing no wounded models.

One thing to note here is that wounded character groups don’t go ahead of non-character groups but you’re not forced to allocate to them first despite being wounded.

Generally this is all pretty easy to understand. Fast rolling saves has been something players “got on with” for several editions but without any codified structure other than the defacto understanding that fast rolling shouldn’t lead to a different outcome than slow rolling. 11th Edition is adding structure to saves as a fundamental concept so that it can then leverage that concept in other rules and abilities.

Precision Attacks24.28§

No rule has leveraged the save groups system more than 11th edition’s version of Precision. This rule is a bit awkwardly worded, and I think many might actually manage to find a read of this that is more complex than is actually intended. It’s fully compatible with the new fast rolling of saves and shouldn’t actually lead to any new groupings, first let's look at the text of the ability one part at a time as it’s got a few moving parts.

While resolving attacks made with one or more [PRECISION] weapons,

So to start us off this ability applies any time precision weapons are used and because of how attack rolls are made in 11th you’ll never have a group of attacks to save against where only some of them have precision and some don’t. The "identical attacks” rule requires all attacks being resolved in a pool of attack dice have the same BS/WS, A, D, AP, and be affected by the same rules and abilities.

...at the start of the Allocation Order step (05.03), if the target unit contains one or more CHARACTER models visible to one or more of the attacking models, the active player can select one allocation group that contains one of those visible CHARACTER models.

This section is a bit clunky in my opinion and I can see people getting confused looking for a more complicated resolution to what is meant to be quite straight forward. If any of the models making those precision attacks can see a character model in the target unit then you can select the allocation group that character is in. They don’t all need to all be able to see the character, this is a pretty big change to how precision worked in 10th edition.

If they do, until those attacks are resolved, or until that CHARACTER group is destroyed (whichever happens first), that CHARACTER group is the current allocation group.

Finally we get to what all this actually does, it forces that chosen character group to become the “current allocation group”, which is a term the rules use for the group being resolved now. It doesn’t stay the current group after these attacks have been resolved though. Let's look at a diagrammed example of this in action.

Attacks that gain Precision via a trigger§

Some attacks can gain precision during the resolution of their attacks, such as if the attack gets a Critical Hit or Critical Wound. How to handle attacks that do this is potentially something we need an FAQ on, I have two distinct views on it as it stands now.

  1. They could just become a sub pool of attacks you resolve as a group as if it was a group of attacks you'd made the normal way. So if you rolled 10 attacks where Critical Hits gained precision, and you rolled four critical hits, and 2 normal hits then you'd resolve this as if you had done them as two separate groups presumably with the active player chosing the order of the groups.
  2. Potentially there's a pretty pedantic read that suggests that such attacks can't be fast rolled, to group attacks they all need to be "identical attacks" and such attacks must be affected by the same rules and abilities. The attacking player can't know which of their attacks will be affected by precision so they'd need to slow roll every single one. Not ideal.

I've seen some suggestions, especially for units that have several sources of attacks like this, that they should pool at the end like Devestation Wounds already do in 11th edition. This seems quite plausible.

Precision into units with more than one Character§

My read of the rule as it stands is that precision attacks only allow you to select a single character group to become the "current allocation group", once that character is destroyed normal allocation would resume. There's no clause to select a second character group if you have some precision attacks left to resolve.

Feel No Pain24.12§

One question I’ve seen repeatedly so far is how this all interacts with the Feel No Pain ability, a rule which was probably one of the most difficult ones to resolve correctly when fast rolling saves in previous editions without adding in some level of advantaged information, especially with regard to re-rolls where fast rolling in previous editions caused you to do so with knowledge of what technically should have been future saves.

This ability always takes the form Feel No Pain X+. Each time a model with this ability would lose a wound, roll one D6: on an X+, that wound is not lost.

In 11th edition, the sequence is actually far far clearer. Saves in the new groups are resolved lowest to highest, as each one is allocated a model and if that model has a Feel No Pain you roll as needed for it at that step before moving on to the next wound roll you’re allocating. If the model survives the attack because the Feel No Pain rolls prevented enough damage, then you can continue allocating to that group/model as normal. Essentially the information advantage is sort of baked as intended now, though only for each set of attacks you're resolving. Remember that Feel No Pain rolls are PER DAMAGE, so if an attack has a Damage characteristic of 3 you need to roll a d6 for each of the 3 wounds it would otherwise inflict.

What about attacks with dX damage?§

Rules as written attacks with damage such as d6+2 should roll for damage when a failed save it allocated to a model, this way you know what number of wounds would be lost to determine how many Feel No Pain rolls to make.

articles.title: Ruleshammer: Terrain Guide
articles.html:
Welcome back to Ruleshammer! In this series we take a detailed look at the rules of key games, but Warhammer 40k in particular. In our first 11th edition article, we're looking at terrain rules in the new edition.

This article primarily deals with terrain as a feature of the board and how it interacts with models' ability to shoot, be shot at, and move. If you’re interested in reading more about how terrain interacts with missions as an objective, please read our review of that concept here. This guide is not a replacement for having read the rules, but should help with a few edge cases that aren’t clear; where necessary I’ve included quotes and references to the rules (such as their new handy rules numbers) to help.

Terrain Areas13.01§

During the placing terrain step of preparing a battlefield for Warhammer 40k 11th edition both players need to agree how each terrain area will be defined. The rules give a few different methods for doing this which the diagram below explains in a bit more detail.

Practically I’d expect terrain area mats to be the predominant method, the areas of specific sizes for the new mission layouts are being sold by GW and are well documented. When using a GW mission layout some touching areas are joined into one larger area, and some are considered separate.

Be aware that every piece of terrain creates a terrain area in 11th.

In each case, the area of the battlefield occupied by that boundary or terrain feature is known as a terrain area. A mission’s deployment map may define the location and dimensions of each terrain area. Otherwise, players must agree on this before the battle.

Why this matters I’ll highlight later on in the guide, but it’s important to note that terrain features that have previously rarely had an area do create one and probably need that area to be shown in some way on your battlefields. Features such as barricades and pipes have had a multitude of rules in the past about how close you need to be to benefit from them, these rules are not present in 11th edition.

Terrain Categories13.02§

Unlike previous editions where one “piece” of terrain had a singular type, terrain features within an terrain area in 11th edition have individually assigned categories of their own. One larger wall might be Dense terrain, whilst a smaller “chest high” (if not Votann or some lesser Tyranids) walls might be Light Terrain. The three types are:

  • Exposed. Craters, Razorwize, Debris and similar.
  • Light. Barricades, Low Walls, and Statues.
  • Dense. Buildings, Ruins, Armoured Containers, and woods.

None of these lists are exhaustive though, so if something on the board doesn’t immediately fall into one of the categories make sure you and your opponent agree on what type it is. This will be important later.

Terrain and Movement13.06§

Movement depends on the keywords of the model you’re moving and the category of the terrain feature they’re trying to move through.

Exposed and Light terrain does not block movement at all for any models.

All models can move horizontally and vertically through exposed and light terrain features.

Dense terrain has a few different ways models interact with it, again based on keywords of the model moving. Some aspects of this are quite different to previous editions.

INFANTRY/BEAST/SWARM/MOBILE can move horizontally through dense terrain features*.*

On the face of it this seems very similar to previous editions but when you consider that dense terrain features are not just walls, but also things like Armoured Containers, or some of the industrial terrain you can buy it’s potentially a pretty significant change.

INFANTRY/BEAST/SWARM models can move vertically through dense terrain features*.*

This rule essentially means models with those keywords can climb ruins and terrain as you’d expect. One element of this that has changed though is that models now need to get within a ½” of any terrain they wish to climb. Fundamentally this just codifies what I think a lot of players already presumed, that you had to actually be near a terrain piece to “climb” it."

Models without the INFANTRY/BEAST/SWARM keywords can’t move through the floors but if they have the FLY or MONSTER keywords they can end a move on upper floors if they meet certain conditions

After ending that move, that model is stable and no part of its base overhangs the outer edge of that surface.

Also take note that FLY models don’t need to fly around floors like they have done in older editions if they “take to the skies”.

Ignore all vertical distance for the purposes of how far it has moved. It can move through all types of model (including enemy models and MONSTER/VEHICLE models). It can move horizontally and vertically through all categories of terrain feature.

For a -2” movement penalty FLY models can move through terrain features like INFANTRY/BEAST/SWARM models can.

None INFANTRY/BEAST/SWARM/FLY/MONSTER models can climb over terrain but cannot end a move above ground level, remember though that this is only necessary if the terrain feature is more than 2” tall. Anything less than 2" tall can be ignored when moving a model, or less than 4" if you have the super heavy walker rule like Imperial Knights.

Terrain and Visibility13.07§

Terrain plays a vital role in Warhammer 40k games and 11th edition iterates on the rules we’re used to in a few key ways, some of them pretty significant changes from previous editions. Arguably this edition represents the biggest change to terrain fundamentals since the obscuring concept was added a few editions ago. Cover Saves which have been a part of 40k in some form since 3rd edition in 1998 are gone, though cover is still very much around! Any 2nd edition players who were around back then and adamant that “cover hit modifiers were fine” in 2nd edition and that “changing it was unnecessary,” errr, congrats I guess?

Benefit of Cover13.08§

11th edition streamlines cover significantly. It doesn’t vary as much between terrain categories as much as it did in 10th. It’s no longer slightly different for ruins, woods, etc. Rather than dwelling on how it worked in the past (which can all now forget) let me explain how it works now!

Key to the new cover mechanic is that it’s a benefit conveyed to units that meet certain conditions, not models!

Each time a ranged attack targets a unit, if every model in that unit meets one or more of the following conditions, that unit has the benefit of cover against that attack:

The first condition applies to specific keyworded units only, but that doesn’t mean units lacking those keywords are excluded from cover, they just need to meet the second criteria.

That model has the INFANTRY/BEAST/SWARM keyword and is within a terrain area.

Note that it’s only within! Not “wholly within”, so “toeing into cover” is possible in 11th.

Vehicles in Cover§

That model is not fully visible to the attacking model due to one or more intervening terrain features and/or one or more intervening obscuring terrain areas.

The first part of this rule is where units without the INFANTRY/BEAST/SWARM keywords can gain cover, they don’t benefit automatically for being within the area. For these units you need to check line of sight and visibility physically.

Almost all terrain you’ll encounter at events and I’d wager most FLGS will be Light or Dense terrain; Pipes, Barricades, Statues, Containers, Ruin Walls and the like. As such they will all be obscuring terrain areas, something I’ll elaborate on further in the next section of this article; the bit that matters here is that if you’re on the other side of an obscuring terrain area you’re out of sight, because it’s obscuring. There is however a type of terrain feature that won’t block Line of Sight that models can gain the benefit of cover from: Exposed terrain features! That’s right, if you find a table somewhere with some razor wire it does provide cover!

What about the “obscuring terrain areas” though?

Obscuring13.09§

Back as a full rule in its own right, obscuring now applies to any terrain area that has one or more light or dense terrain features. It’s not something players need to decide on per terrain area as they’ve been required to do in the past. Some terrain categories that weren’t line of sight blocking in previous editions such as woods can block line of sight in 11th. Note that while woods are not featured on the GW mission layouts you can replace any dense terrain feature with a tree in this edition without any significant change to how that terrain area functions.

Obscuring and Cover§

One of the subtle changes between the obscuring we’ve seen in the past is the way obscuring terrain areas can convey the benefit of cover, even if no terrain features actually cause the model partially behind the terrain area to be not fully visible.

What if two units are partially within the same terrain area?§

Units attacking accross the same terrain area can still gain cover in 11th edition, but cover and and obscuring are per model checks! You need to consider what is visible for each attacking model to determine if the cover penalty applies. Here's an example.

You might have seen a version of this diagram with Skitarii, turns out Skitarii ignore cover with an ability so they were a bad example.

Obscuring and Line of Sight§

If every line of sight drawn between two models crosses one or more obscuring terrain areas (excluding obscuring terrain areas that one or both of those models are within), those two models are not visible to each other.

Remember that the obscuring rule doesn’t apply if both units are within the same obscuring terrain area. Yes, obscuring does still block line of sight between terrain areas in ways that can seem unintuitive, such as blocking line of sight between units both on the top floor of different terrain areas with an area of lower obscuring terrain between them. All editions of Warhammer 40k require a level of abstraction to make the game work well as a game. If you want to imagine the buildings are on fire, or that artillery bombardment has created a dust cloud limiting your units line of sight, please do!

Joined and Unjoined Terrain Areas and Obscuring§

Pay close attention during terrain step up to which areas are joined and which are not as the impact this has on how obscuring areas function is significant. Here's some examples to help.

What about Towering and Aircraft?§

In 11th edition the TOWERING and AIRCRAFT rules have no impact on how obscuring functions. Every unit can see normally through obscuring terrain areas if within (not wholly within) the terrain area, and AIRCRAFT can be blocked from line of sight by it like all other models.

Solid13.11§

This is a new rule for 11th edition, but is also really just GW codifying a very common tournament house rule. It does two things but they’re frustratingly covered in two different parts of the core rules. The main rule itself is about Line of Sight.

Dense terrain features have the Solid rule. Line of sight cannot be drawn across any enclosed gap in the surface of such a terrain feature that is 3" or less from ground level.

The other element of this rule though is as much about movement as it is about Line of Sight; found on 48 nestled into the sidebar Solid has this additional effect:

If a terrain feature has the Solid rule (13.11), as dense terrain features do, a model cannot end a move such that any part of it is through any enclosed part of that terrain feature that is 3" or less from ground level – not even through small openings such as doors and windows. This ensures that protruding elements of models cannot be used to circumvent the visibility restrictions of the Solid rule.

So you can’t stick a gun barrel through a window or door in 11th edition and gain line of sight through the wall that way! It also means you explicitly can’t end a move with a model part way through these features, even if the doors and gaps might allow you to.

What about Hidden?§

Although hidden is a rule that involves terrain, it's more about visibility and has a whole host of edge cases and issues which are covered here in our Ruleshammer Guide for Hidden and Gone to Ground.
