articles.title: Ruleshammer: Hidden and Gone To Ground Guide
articles.html:
Welcome back to Ruleshammer! In this series we take a detailed look at the rules of key games, but Warhammer 40k in particular. In our second 11th edition article, we're looking at the new hidden and gone to ground rules from the new edition.

Hidden is one of the new fundamental rules for Warhammer 40k 11th edition; this guide should bring you up to speed on how Hidden works as well as explaining the new Gone To Ground rule added in the 11th Edition Rules Commentary.

How to Be Hidden13.09§

A model is hidden while all of the following apply to it:

  • That model has the INFANTRY/BEASTS/SWARM keyword and is within a terrain area that contains one or more light/dense terrain features.
  • That model’s unit did not make one or more ranged attacks during this turn or during the previous turn.

This bit is fairly straight forward: Don’t shoot, be INFANTRY/BEAST/SWARM, and be within a terrain area that has at least one light/dense terrain feature, and the model gets to be hidden.

This version of the hidden rule is from the 11th edition rules commentary and is slightly different to the printed version in the Core Rules PDF. Models can now be hidden in terrain areas which only have light terrain features, compared to the original version in the core rules that only applied if the terrain area had any dense terrain features. If using the Games Workshop mission terrain layouts this change means that a model can be hidden in every terrain area on the board, none of them fail to meet this criteria. In fact exposed terrain features don’t appear to be used in the official layouts at all.

Note that you can be hidden during the first turn, there's a specific entry in the Rules Commentary that confirms that even though you haven't had a previous turn yet, rules that require "something to not have happened" still function. Hidden gets a specific mention.

Hidden and the First Turn13.09.01§

During the first turn, conditions that require something to not have happened during the previous turn are true. For the Hidden rule, this means that during the first turn a unit has not made ranged attacks during the previous turn.

What Does Hidden Do?§

While a model is hidden, it can only be visible to enemy models that are within its detection range. Unless otherwise stated, a model’s detection range is 15".

Hidden causes a model to count as not visible to attacking models outside of detection range regardless of if any terrain features or obscuring terrain areas area blocking the model from view. Note that it's models not units, it's possible for some models in a unit to not be hidden whilst others are. In scuh a case hidden will only be of benefit if the only models that would have been visible are hidden, if the unit can target any non-hidden models (i.e. they're visible and in range) then the rule offers no protection. Detection range is by default 15” but we’ve already seen a few rules that can increase this and decrease this in some of the Warhammer Community previews.

Remember that this rule doesn’t change line of sight rules generally. If a hidden model is within detection range but not visible due to intervening terrain features or obscuring terrain areas then it remains not visible as normal.

One thing to note here as well though is that detection range has limits in the Rules Commentary on how much it can be modified.

Detection Range and Lone Operative can not be better than 9" or worse than 30".

Gone to Ground13.11.01§

Gone To Ground adds additional interactions between Solid [13.11] terrain features and the Hidden rules. This rule can be found in the Rules Commentary in the 40k App, but here’s the text to aid explanation here.

Hidden models that are within Solid terrain features gain an extra benefit while they have gone to ground.

This whole sentence is a bit odd already and probably needs a revision, models can't be "within" a solid terrain feature. They can be within a terrain area that has solid terrain features though, and I think it's pretty clear this is what's meant, but it's still odd to refer to them this way as Solid isn't a category of terrain feature. Solid is a rule ALL dense terrain features gain, you can read more about this in the terrain guide.

A model has gone to ground while all of the following apply

  • That model is hidden.
  • That model is not fully visible to the attacking model due to one or more intervening solid terrain features.
  • That model's unit did not make one or more ranged attacks during this turn or the previous turn.

While a model has gone to ground, subtract 3" from that model's detection range. Designers Note: Units that made ranged attacks in the current or previous turn cannot go to ground regardless of any abilities that allow them to shoot and remain hidden.

Essentially this rule reduces detection range for models that are not fully visible to the attacking model. It applies to specific models against specific attacks. Note however that despite Hidden being extended to include light terrain features, this rule only applies "if a model is not fully visible due to one or more intervening solid terrain features". Light terrain features are not solid so they can't provide the gone to ground benefit.

Because this rule relies on solid terrain features one of the key limitations you might encounter with it in your games is that quite often a unit that would have the gone to ground benefit, is not visible anyway because it’s entirely behind a solid piece of terrain.

This does mean that unlike hidden, gone to ground is not a true or false state like being hidden is. It depends on the perspective of the attacking models and intervening solid terrain features.

articles.title: Hammer of Math: The Benefit of Cover
articles.html:
In Hammer of Math, we look at various statistical concepts in Warhammer 40k, looking at how to evaluate different options mathematically and understanding how those probabilities and statistics affect your games. In this article we're looking at how the changes to cover affect weapons and damage output.

Welcome back to Hammer of Math! It's been a while, hasn't it? We stopped posting for a while in part because I didn't have time for these and Primaris Kevin had stopped playing 40k and in part because we felt like we'd covered all of the key topics 10th edition had to offer. But now we've got an entirely new edition with entirely new math to talk about, and we should start with the biggest, gnarliest topic: Cover.

In tenth edition, the benefit of cover was +1 to your save, with some exceptions for models with 3+ and 2+ saves. In 11th edition, the benefit of cover has changed to being -1 BS for the attacking model, making your attacks less accurate. This has interesting knock-on effects for almost every weapon and unit in the game.

The Value of Cover Is Variable§

So let's start with the mathematical realities of the situation: In many situations, -1 BS for the attacking unit and +1 to saves will feel about the same. Take for example a unit of five Intercessors dumping 20 shots into a unit of ten Guardsmen.

In tenth edition, the Intercessors will hit with two-thirds (67%) of their attacks, hitting on 3+. They'll wound the same percentage (67%), as they wound on 3+, and the Guardsmen in cover will be +1 to their saves but suffer -1 AP from the bolt rifles, so they're saving on a 5+, or one third (33%) of the time. If we want to predict how many dead Guardsmen this gives us, the math is:

Shots \ (chance to hit) \ (chance to wound) * (chance of an unsaved wound)

Now one thing to note here is that that thing up there is a product - it's four values multiplied by each other. Multiplication is commutative, so you can change the order of those values without changing the answer. In this case it'll be:

20 \ (2/3) \ (2/3) \ (2/3) = 20 \ (8/27) = 5.93

So we're looking an an average of around six dead Guardsmen. Now let's adjust the numbers for 11th edition. Now we're hitting on 4+ (50%), the Guardsmen's save will be modified to a 6+ (17%). Let's look at the math:

20 \ (1/2) \ (2/3) \ (5/6) = 20 \ (10/36) = 5.56

You are not going to feel that difference most of the time. Yes, 5.9 is higher than 5.6 but when those shots resolve, you're likely looking at 5-7 dead Guardsmen in either scenario.

In this case, I picked Guardsmen because it matters that they have a 5+ save. If I had picked Kasrkin or a unit with a 4+ save, the math would have instead been:

10th: 20 \ (2/3) \ (2/3) \ (1/2) = 20 \ (4/18) = 4.4

11th: 20 \ (1/2) \ (2/3) \ (2/3) = 20 \ (4/18) = 4.4

Making the result identical. Similarly, if we'd started with a 3+ save against AP-1 shooting, we'd have seen a marked improvement, as previously we'd expect to inflict 3 wounds against our imaginary Adepta Sororitas targets and now we can expect to see 3.3 pushed through. And this all assumes we're starting with BS 3+; if we start with BS 4+, our effectiveness drops more substantially.

That's because the value of +1 or -1 tends to be dependent on the value you start with and how big a percentage change that represents. Going from a 3+ to hit to a 2+ (an increase in accuracy of 25%) is not as big a deal as going from a 5+ to a 4+, where you get a 50% increase in accuracy. This ends up playing a major role in who benefits the most from 11th edition cover rules. This is also a handy time to go back to a chart we posted from the very first Hammer of Math:

RollInitial Probability-1 Modifier ProbabilityDelta+1 Modifier ProbabilityDelta
2+83%67%-20%83%+0%
3+67%50%-25%83%+25%
4+50%33%-33%67%+33%
5+33%17%-50%50%+50%
6+17%0%-100%33%+100%

The value of re-rolls is a bit different in that it's multiplicative and so their value does not vary based on the starting value.

What This Means: AP -1 Weapons Really Matter Now§

So to recap here: -1 to your Ballistic Skill is more impactful the worse your starting BS was, and improving saves is more impactful the worse your starting save was. Weapons which hit on a 3+ with AP-1, such as heavy bolters and autocannons in Marine armies, will see the biggest impacts here.

Heavy Bolters get an 11% bump when you factor in the impact of [SUSTAINED HITS 1] here, though again your value will depend on your starting Ballistic Skill. That said, if you're looking at Guardsmen, using Take Aim! ahead of time will give you similar results. Getting any AP at all is a bigger improvement than being more likely to hit for these weapons.

The Other Major Factors§

There are two other key factors here to consider, which can make doing the math behind cover in 11th edition tricky:

  • Hit Rolls have a floor. That is, a roll of a 6 to hit always hits, regardless of modifiers, but a roll of a 6 to save does not always save - it's often possible to have an impossible save roll. This means AP modifiers can often be more impactful than hit modifiers.
  • Invulnerable saves. The other major factor is invulnerable saves, which aren't affected by cover and cap the value of AP to begin with. Chaos Daemons in particular only have invulnerable saves and so the new cover rules are a flat upgrade for the army as an entire faction.

To get an idea of how these factors change things let's look at the main gun choices for the Chaos Space Marines Defiler: The Hades Lascannon and the Heavy Reaper Autocannon. The Hades has 2 shots at BS 3+, S13, AP-3 with D6+1 damage, while the Heavy Reaper is four shots at S9, AP-1, 3 damage with SUSTAINED HITS 1 and DEVASTATING WOUNDS.

Here the Hades Lascannon shooting into Terminator targets (or any target with a 2+/4+ save and 3 wounds), sees a noticeable drop in value in 11th edition thanks to the fact that the AP situation just didn't matter. Meanwhile the Autocannon just gets a substantial lift, becoming a more effective option given the extra shots - and that's before you factor in Devastating Wounds or the fact that the Lascannon will occasionally do 2 damage and not kill a Terminator.

In this last example, the Lascannon comes out on top at BS 3+ but quickly drops off as the number of extra shots from the Reaper helps it catch up (and again, this does not include devastating wounds). And even then, the difference is much, much smaller than it used to be - the 10th edition double lascannon delivered double or more of the value of the autocannon even with half the shots, albeit with the occasional risk of rolling low on damage.

Final Thoughts§

Ultimately, the big winners here are models with good invulnerable saves and weapons with a high volume of AP-1 shooting, as those are the units which see the largest net benefit from the new cover rules. Meanwhile low-volume, high-"value" shooting weapons like multi-meltas and lascannons see a comparative drop, as they both become more volatile and less effective - and that will be even more true for units with worse starting Ballistic Skill characteristics. Being able to do D6+1 damage will still have value - the twin lascannon in our example still out-paced the Heavy Reaper Autocannon for shooting units with a starting Ballistic Skill of 4+ or better - but you're going to have to weigh that against the higher chance of just whiffing your shots.

There are a number of other more specific examples we can look at in the coming weeks, and you can expect us to do that, diving into how these changes affect specific units and their weapon options.

toc.title: The Value of Cover Is Variable
What This Means: AP -1 Weapons Really Matter Now

articles.title: Hammer of Math: Coherency, Circles, and Triangles
articles.html:
In Hammer of Math, we look at various statistical concepts in Warhammer 40k, looking at how to evaluate different options mathematically and understanding how those probabilities and statistics affect your games. In this article we're looking at coherency rules in 11th edition and some unique geometric quirks which can help you in ways you might not have expected.

Welcome back to another 11th edition installment of Hammer of Math. Last Time Around we talked about the new rules for cover in 11th edition, and how those might affect your weapon selection, seeing that higher volumes of low AP attacks can yield better results. This time around we're talking about the new coherency rules and secant lines. For those of you who need the refresher from high school geometry, a secant line is a straight line that passes through a circle and intersects it at exactly two distinct points.

The big change the new coherency rules established in 11th edition was a maximum range for your models with regard to Coherency. In tenth edition if you had more than six models in your unit, every model had to be within 2" of at least two other models. This meant that you could string units out across incredibly long distances, so long as you put a triangle-shaped configuration of models on each end of the line to maintain that 2" of two models requirement.

11th edition makes a key change to this by removing the "two models" requirement but making it so that no model in the unit can be more than 9" from any other model. This effectively puts a maximum radius on how much space units can occupy, though the changes to "within" this edition - specifically removing most instances of "wholly within" means you can set a model on the outer edge of the ring and get interesting with your spacing. In the example below, I've stretched out a unit of 25mm bases, representing the maximum area you can block off with a unit of ten Poxwalkers.

This gives you around 10.8" of total width for blocking, though remember that in 11th edition models can move through your engagement range, they just can't end inside it. Interestingly this means you can now block of wider areas with small units on 40mm bases, since they can overhang the edge of your coherency circle by a larger amount, and filling a 9" across gap with 2" spacing can be done with three models.

Spread across this way you can max out around 12" in width for your area blocking, and things can get even crazier when you consider things like 80mm oval bases for units like bikes - Outriders can do some silly things with the new coherency rules.

Of course, in the rush to create new game aids, we've seen a lot of 9" circular tools repurposed for tracking unit coherency. These are powerful tools, and convenient to use, but they don't account for all of the ways you can set up in coherency. There's a fun edge case using two secant lines where you can set your units up and get a wider coverage area:

In this special case, the three corner units of our equilateral triangle are all within 9" of each other. Note that this won't work for polygons with more sides; a 9" square will have a diagonal of 12.72".

So where is this helpful? Well the reason you go through all of these thought exercises around is because you need to screen units out in the Movement phase. If you can stop a unit from touching an objective in the Movement phase, then you can prevent them from performing actions on that objective while forcing them to charge you in the Charge phase in order to be able to actually capture that objective. If they merely shoot your unit to death, they won't be on the objective, and won't score for holding it, either at the end of their turn or the start of their next Command phase.

The change to 2" Engagement Ranges makes this much easier, though with the caveat that smaller models can walk through those ranges and the little bump-outs on 11th edition terrain can make this more of a chore.

In this example we have a coherent unit of six models on 40mm bases screening out an entire large area terrain feature. When you are doing this the corners need special attention - you have to be closer to the sides in order to avoid leaving room for a unit to "toe in" on the corner of the terrain feature. Going back to our note about the diagonal of a square being longer than the sides, if you place 2" in from each side, you'll be about 3.17" in from the corner.

The name of the game here is using space effectively. Having a 2" Engagement Range means more units can fight but it also makes screening enemy units out way easier and way more important, provided you can stop them from ending up on the other side of you - which is easy to do with ruins and area terrain where any gap less than 3" wide won't fit a model, even on a 25mm base.

That said, the one thing to remember here is that your opponent can always just charge you, either using the Heroic Intervention core stratagem or just a regular charge in the Charge phase so presumably if you're doing this, it's with the idea that you'll win that particular combat with your superior, 40mm-based units and put them in an even worse position.

Final Thoughts§

This was a fun one; we don't get to talk too much about geometry on Hammer of Math and so doing some of the math behind coherency and screening opens up some fun possibilities. Next week we'll revisit some of the basic statistics of Warhammer 40k and refresh one of our older articles, talking about how those concepts apply to 11th edition.
